License: arXiv.org perpetual non-exclusive license
arXiv:2604.26950v1 [math.DG] 29 Apr 2026

Weighted Linearization of Vector Fields
via a Formal Moser Trick

Arthur Lei Qiu
Abstract

Many well-known theorems establish sufficient criteria for linearizability of a vector field in terms of the eigenvalues of its linear approximation. By attaching weights to coordinates so that some directions are considered “linear”, others “quadratic”, and so on, one can define the notion of a weighted linear approximation. It is thus natural to ask when a vector field is “weighted-linearizable”. In this paper, we formulate a weighted version of the non-resonance condition appearing in the Poincaré and Sternberg linearization theorems and show that it implies weighted linearizability.

Our approach first addresses weighted linearization on the level of formal power series. In doing so, we develop a general framework to make sense of a power series version of Moser’s trick, a technique used to prove various normal form results in geometry. This formal Moser trick works over any field of characteristic zero and may be of independent interest.

Contents
  1. 1 Introduction
  2. 2 Formal geometry
  3. 3 Weightings of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n}
  4. 4 Weighted linearization
  5. References

1 Introduction

1.1 Background

Linearization of vector fields is a fundamental technique with applications throughout mathematics, science, and engineering. It features prominently in the study of local normal forms in differential geometry and dynamical systems, where a natural question arises: when does the first-order approximation furnished by linearizing a vector field accurately reflect the original vector field’s geometry and dynamics in the vicinity of a fixed point? This question is classical, dating back to Poincaré and Lie, and by now there is a wealth of literature establishing sufficient conditions for linearizability with respect to various notions of “accuracy”.

In the topological category, the Hartman–Grobman theorem [7, §4.3] states that the flow of a C1C^{1} vector field on ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n} can be conjugated via a homeomorphism to the flow of its linear approximation near a hyperbolic fixed point (one where the linear approximation has no purely imaginary eigenvalues). On the level of formal power series, Poincaré’s thesis [22] investigates when a system of nn ordinary differential equations

x˙=A​x+O​(|x|2),A∈ℂn×n\displaystyle\dot{x}=Ax+O(|x|^{2}),\qquad A\in\mathbb{C}^{n\times n} (1)

can be brought to the form y˙=A​y\dot{y}=Ay by a formal change of coordinates y=x+O​(|x|2)y=x+O(|x|^{2}). He formulates the following sufficient condition: call the eigenvalues λ1,…,λn\lambda_{1},\ldots,\lambda_{n} of the matrix AA resonant if there exists k∈{1,…,n}k\in\{1,\ldots,n\} and non-negative integers α1,…,αn\alpha_{1},\ldots,\alpha_{n} such that

⟨λ,α⟩=λkand∑j=1nαj>1,where⟨λ,α⟩:=∑j=1nλj​αj.\displaystyle\langle\lambda,\alpha\rangle=\lambda_{k}\quad\text{and}\quad\sum_{j=1}^{n}\alpha_{j}>1,\quad\text{where}\quad\langle\lambda,\alpha\rangle:=\sum_{j=1}^{n}\lambda_{j}\alpha_{j}.

Poincaré’s linearization theorem then states that, if the eigenvalues are non(!)-resonant, then such a formal change of coordinates exists. Should the formal power series on the right-hand side of Equation (1) define a holomorphic vector field on ℂn\mathbb{C}^{n}, results of Poincaré and Siegel describe when a linearizing formal coordinate transformation converges to a biholomorphism, again in terms of eigenvalues of AA [2, §24].

In the smooth (by which we always mean C∞C^{\infty}) category, Sternberg’s linearization theorem [23, 24] states that a smooth vector field on ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n} with a fixed point at the origin can be linearized by a diffeomorphism provided that the (possibly complex) eigenvalues of its Jacobian matrix at the origin satisfy the same non-resonance condition as in Poincaré’s linearization theorem. For example, fix an integer m>1m>1 and consider the vector field on ℝ2\mathbb{R}^{2} given by

X=(x+ym)​∂∂x+y​∂∂y.\displaystyle X=(x+y^{m})\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}}+y\frac{\partial}{\partial{y}}.

This vector field is Euler-like, meaning that its linear approximation at 0 is the Euler vector field ℰ=z​∂∂z+w​∂∂w\mathcal{E}=z\frac{\partial}{\partial{z}}+w\frac{\partial}{\partial{w}}. Since ℰ\mathcal{E} has non-resonant eigenvalues (λ1,λ2)=(1,1)(\lambda_{1},\lambda_{2})=(1,1), Sternberg’s theorem ensures that there exists a smooth local change of coordinates z=z​(x,y)z=z(x,y), w=w​(x,y)w=w(x,y) transforming XX into ℰ\mathcal{E} near 0. In fact, one can explicitly check that the (global) change of coordinates z=x−1m−1​ymz=x-\frac{1}{m-1}y^{m}, w=yw=y works. Euler-like vector fields can be defined more generally along a submanifold of a smooth manifold as those vector fields whose linear approximation is equal to the Euler vector field of the submanifold’s normal bundle; Bursztyn–Lima–Meinrenken [5, Lemma 2.4] have proved that every Euler-like vector field is linearizable along the entire submanifold.

In contrast, neither Sternberg’s nor Poincaré’s theorem can be applied to the weighted Euler-like vector field

Y=(x+ym)​∂∂x+2​y​∂∂y,\displaystyle Y=(x+y^{m})\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}}+2y\frac{\partial}{\partial{y}}, (2)

as its linear part has resonant eigenvalues (λ1,λ2)=(1,2)(\lambda_{1},\lambda_{2})=(1,2). Nevertheless, YY can be smoothly linearized: the coordinates u=x−12​m−1​ymu=x-\frac{1}{2m-1}y^{m}, v=yv=y take YY to the weighted Euler vector field u​∂∂u+2​v​∂∂vu\frac{\partial}{\partial{u}}+2v\frac{\partial}{\partial{v}}. Actually, this coordinate transformation does the job even when m=1m=1, in which case the linear term y​∂∂xy\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}} disappears upon changing coordinates. More generally, Meinrenken has proved [17, Lemma 2.5] that every weighted Euler-like vector field is smoothly linearizable, even though many such vector fields are resonant.

Thus, while the non-resonance condition is sufficient for formal and smooth linearizability, it is far from necessary. For resonant vector fields, Poincaré’s theorem is extended by the Poincaré–Dulac theorem [2, §23], which produces a formal change of coordinates that might remove some but not all non-linear terms in the vector field. The particular non-linearities that can appear upon changing coordinates depend on certain non-canonical choices made during the iterative construction of the coordinate transformation, and these in turn depend on the particular resonance relations occurring in the vector field, so there is some ambiguity in what the resulting normal form looks like.

This paper offers one systematic way to gain more control: by accepting a generalized notion of linearity. For instance, one can assign different positive integer weights to the coordinates of ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n} or ℂn\mathbb{C}^{n}, so that coordinates of weight 11 are considered “linear”, coordinates of weight 22 are considered “quadratic”, and so on. The weighted Euler vector field u​∂∂u+2​v​∂∂vu\frac{\partial}{\partial{u}}+2v\frac{\partial}{\partial{v}} from before corresponds to assigning weights (w1,w2)=(1,2)(w_{1},w_{2})=(1,2) to the first and second coordinates of ℝ2\mathbb{R}^{2}, respectively, which explains why the coordinate transformation (x,y)↦(u,v)(x,y)\mapsto(u,v) removes the linear term y​∂∂xy\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}} from the vector field (2) when m=1m=1: it is considered “non-linear” with respect to these weights.

Such weightings appear throughout mathematics. For instance, weighted projective spaces are objects of study in algebraic geometry, and Loizides–Meinrenken [15] have recently developed a differential-geometric theory of weightings. Weighted linear approximations of vector fields have been previously studied by Algaba–García–Reyes [1] under the name of “like-linearizations”. Weighted Euler-like vector fields play an important role in their work, which provides necessary and sufficient conditions for a smooth vector field to be equivalent to its weighted linear approximation in two different senses: the vector fields themselves being related by a diffeomorphism, and their flows being orbitally equivalent (i.e., the same up to time reparametrization). Meinrenken’s [17, Theorem 5.10] can be seen as a geometric incarnation of their [1, Theorem 1.2].

1.2 Main results

Algaba–García–Reyes’ characterization of weighted linearizability differs from the other linearization theorems discussed in Section 1.1 in that it is not expressed in terms of eigenvalues, which are straightforward to compute in principle. Our goal is to give an eigenvalue-based condition that implies weighted linearizability, akin to the non-resonance condition of Poincaré’s and Sternberg’s linearization theorems. For a given weighting, our weighted non-resonance condition is more permissive than the usual non-resonance condition. The compromise is that weighted linear approximations need not be linear in the usual sense; nevertheless, one has more control over the non-linear terms that appear compared to the Poincaré–Dulac theorem, as the non-linearities must be “weighted-linear”, and one is free to choose the weighting to their advantage.

By combining the main results of this paper, we offer the following weighted generalization of the Poincaré and Sternberg linearization theorems.

Theorem 1.1.

Let XX be either a formal vector field on 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} where 𝕂\mathbb{K} is any field of characteristic zero, or a smooth vector field on an open neighbourhood of 0∈ℝn0\in\mathbb{R}^{n}. If w=(w1,…,wn)w=(w_{1},\ldots,w_{n}) is any weighting of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} (resp. ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n}) with respect to which XX is “admissible” and “non-resonant” (Definitions 4.1 and 4.9), then there exists a formal (resp. germ of a smooth) diffeomorphism that conjugates XX with its weighted linear approximation with respect to ww.

A special case of this theorem (Proposition 4.2) easily detects that the ym​∂∂xy^{m}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}} term in the vector field (2) can be removed when Poincaré’s and Sternberg’s theorems could not (though one can already see this from [17, Lemma 2.5]).

To explain our approach, it is worth stepping back from weighted linearizability and considering a more abstract problem. A fundamental question in differential geometry is: given tensor fields A0A_{0} and A1A_{1} (or differential forms, or multivector fields) on a smooth manifold MM, when does there exist a diffeomorphism φ:M→M\varphi\colon M\to M such that the pullback φ∗​A1\varphi^{*}A_{1} is equal to A0A_{0}? If not globally, one can at least ask: if x∈Mx\in M is a point such that A0​(x)=A1​(x)A_{0}(x)=A_{1}(x), when does there exist a locally defined diffeomorphism φ\varphi such that φ​(x)=x\varphi(x)=x and φ∗​A1=A0\varphi^{*}A_{1}=A_{0} nearby?

Moser’s trick [16, §3.2] is a powerful technique for answering this question. In the broadest sense, it consists of searching for a one-parameter family of tensor fields (or differential forms, or multivector fields) {At}t∈[0,1]\{A_{t}\}_{t\in[0,1]} joining A0A_{0} and A1A_{1} and a one-parameter family of diffeomorphisms {φt}t∈[0,1]\{\varphi_{t}\}_{t\in[0,1]} such that φ0\varphi_{0} is the identity map and dd​t​(φt∗​At)=0\frac{d}{dt}(\varphi_{t}^{*}A_{t})=0. If such families can be found, then φ1∗​A1=φ0∗​A0=A0\varphi_{1}^{*}A_{1}=\varphi_{0}^{*}A_{0}=A_{0}, so φ:=φ1\varphi:=\varphi_{1} is such a diffeomorphism. Typically, instead of directly looking for the family of diffeomorphisms φt\varphi_{t}, one seeks out the time-dependent vector field XtX_{t} that generates φt\varphi_{t} as its flow. The rate of change of φt∗​At\varphi_{t}^{*}A_{t} is then related to the Lie derivative ℒXt\mathcal{L}_{X_{t}} via the equation

dd​t​(φt∗​At)=φt∗​(d​Atd​t+ℒXt​At).\displaystyle\frac{d}{dt}\big(\varphi_{t}^{*}A_{t})=\varphi_{t}^{*}\left(\frac{dA_{t}}{dt}+\mathcal{L}_{X_{t}}A_{t}\right). (3)

One can often make a judicious choice of the interpolating family AtA_{t} based on the particular structure of a given problem, thus finding a solution XtX_{t} to the differential equation d​Atd​t+ℒXt​At=0\frac{dA_{t}}{dt}+\mathcal{L}_{X_{t}}A_{t}=0 that can be integrated to produce the desired φt\varphi_{t}. Originally developed by Moser in the context of volume-preserving geometry [19], Moser’s trick can be used to give very short proofs of the Darboux theorem [25] and Morse lemma [21]; it also facilitates Bursztyn–Lima–Meinrenken’s proof that Euler-like vector fields are linearizable along submanifolds, and Meinrenken’s proof that weighted Euler-like vector fields are weighted-linearizable.

Techniques used in Sternberg’s linearization theorem were later modified by Chen [6] to prove:

{named*}

[Chen’s theorem of equivalence] Suppose XX and YY are smooth vector fields that both have a hyperbolic fixed point at 0∈ℝn0\in\mathbb{R}^{n}. Let X~\widetilde{X} and Y~\widetilde{Y} denote the formal vector fields obtained by replacing each component function of XX and YY with its formal Taylor series centred at 0. Then there exists a (germ of a) diffeomorphism fixing 0 that takes XX to YY if and only if there exists a formal change of coordinates that takes X~\widetilde{X} to Y~\widetilde{Y}.

Since complex roots of polynomials with real coefficients come in conjugate pairs, non-resonance implies hyperbolicity for smooth vector fields: if a fixed point is not hyperbolic, then either 0 is an eigenvalue (in which case 0=2⋅00=2\cdot 0 is a resonance), or there is a pair of non-zero purely imaginary eigenvalues ±i​θ\pm i\theta (in which case i​θ=2​(i​θ)+1​(−i​θ)i\theta=2(i\theta)+1(-i\theta) is a resonance). Thus, one way to linearize a non-resonant smooth vector field is by Taylor expanding its component functions. Poincaré’s theorem then produces a formal diffeomorphism that kills off any higher finite-order terms; Chen’s theorem upgrades this to an actual diffeomorphism that kills off any remaining infinite-order terms.

In view of Chen’s theorem of equivalence, we sought to develop a Moser-type argument for Theorem 1.1 that passes through the world of formal power series. Working with functions and vector fields on ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n} or ℂn\mathbb{C}^{n} only insofar as their formal Taylor series mattered led us to wonder if the time parameter tt could be treated as a formal variable as well. One obvious problem arises in the context of Moser’s trick: namely, how to “evaluate” an arbitrary power series at t=1t=1. Instead of giving up and treating tt as a real or complex parameter, we obstinately proceeded to develop criteria describing when it does make sense to do so. In the process, we realized that many of our results do not use any properties about ℝ\mathbb{R} or ℂ\mathbb{C} besides that they are fields of characteristic zero. We obtain a “Moser trick for formal power series” in contexts beyond its original geometric scope:

{named*}

[Formal Moser trick (Corollary 2.24)] Let A0A_{0} and A1A_{1} be formal tensor fields. Suppose there exists an “evaluative” (Definition 2.19) time-dependent formal tensor field AtA_{t} such that At|t=0=A0A_{t}|_{t=0}=A_{0} and At|t=1=A1A_{t}|_{t=1}=A_{1}, and there exists a time-dependent formal vector field XtX_{t} satisfying Equation (3) in a certain formal sense and whose flow is “evaluative”. Then there exists a formal diffeomorphism φ\varphi such that φ∗​A1=A0\varphi^{*}A_{1}=A_{0}.

1.3 Paper overview

The rest of this paper is structured as follows. In Sections 2.1 and 2.2, we define formal algebraic analogues of standard differential-geometric concepts: vector/tensor fields, diffeomorphisms, related constructions, and one-parameter families thereof. In Section 2.3, we state and prove a version of the existence and uniqueness theorem for solutions of ordinary differential equations (Theorem 2.8) that allows us to import the equation d​Atd​t+ℒXt​At=0\frac{dA_{t}}{dt}+\mathcal{L}_{X_{t}}A_{t}=0 needed for Moser’s trick into this formal setting. We introduce the notion of “evaluative” formal objects in Section 2.4 and give sufficient conditions for formal one-parameter families of objects to be evaluative.

Section 3.1 introduces weightings within the formal world and notes some comparisons to the differentiable world. Section 3.2 generalizes the sufficient conditions for “evaluability” from Section 2.4 to account for such weightings.

Section 4.1 defines the class of vector fields for which it makes sense to pose the weighted linearizability question; offers an informal argument in the smooth world yielding a plausibly sufficient condition for weighted linearizability; then turns this argument on its head to prove that this condition is indeed enough for weighted linearizability on the level of formal power series (Theorem 4.4). This condition, phrased in terms of invertibility of a certain linear (in the usual sense) operator defined by a given vector field, at first looks nothing like the eigenvalue-based conditions of the linearization theorems previously described. We bridge the connection in Section 4.2 by stating our weighted non-resonance condition and proving it implies that this linear operator is invertible (Theorem 4.10), thus yielding a sufficient condition for formal weighted linearizability. The paper concludes in Section 4.3 by explaining how Chen’s theorem of equivalence can still be invoked in the weighted setting to upgrade from formal to smooth weighted linearization (Corollary 4.15).

Throughout this paper, 𝕂\mathbb{K} denotes a fixed choice of any field of characteristic zero. Algebras and algebra homomorphisms are required to be unital; derivations are required to be 𝕂\mathbb{K}-linear. We include 0 in the set of natural numbers ℕ:={0,1,2,…}\mathbb{N}:=\{0,1,2,\ldots\} to avoid overusing super-/subscripts. A multi-index of length kk is a kk-tuple α∈ℕk\alpha\in\mathbb{N}^{k}; its degree is denoted by |α|:=∑iαi|\alpha|:=\sum_{i}\alpha_{i}.

1.4 Acknowledgements

I am greatly indebted to Eckhard Meinrenken for his diligent mentorship while supervising my Master’s project, of which the present work is an expansion. I am also deeply grateful to Yael Karshon for insightful comments and patient support throughout the preparation of this paper. Feedback from Nic Fellini and Charlie Wu has been instrumental in the process of writing and editing the manuscript.

This work was partially funded by a University of Toronto Excellence Award, an Ontario Graduate Scholarship, and a doctoral NSERC Canada Graduate Research Scholarship.

2 Formal geometry

We begin by laying out the framework in which we shall obtain a formal version of Moser’s trick. Much of the material in this section may be well-known—perhaps even obvious—but we include it for the sake of making this paper self-contained and setting the notation used throughout. We have made an effort to highlight connections to various related concepts in geometry and algebra; in particular, to poly-flow vector fields and locally nilpotent derivations (Remarks 2.30 and 2.31).

2.1 Formal functions, diffeomorphisms, and tensor fields

We write 𝕂​[[x]]:=𝕂​[[x1,…,xn]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]:=\mathbb{K}[\![x^{1},\ldots,x^{n}]\!] for the 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra of formal power series in nn commuting variables x1,…,xnx^{1},\ldots,x^{n} with coefficients in 𝕂\mathbb{K}. Elements of 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] are expressions of the form

∑α∈ℕncα​xα,where ​cα∈𝕂​ and ​xα:=(x1)α1​⋯​(xn)αn.\displaystyle\sum_{\alpha\in\mathbb{N}^{n}}c_{\alpha}x^{\alpha},\qquad\text{where }c_{\alpha}\in\mathbb{K}\text{ and }x^{\alpha}:=(x^{1})^{\alpha_{1}}\cdots(x^{n})^{\alpha_{n}}.

Such expressions are intended to be evocative of the Taylor series of (germs of) smooth functions,111This is true on the nose for 𝕂=ℝ\mathbb{K}=\mathbb{R}, as the ℝ\mathbb{R}-algebra homomorphism C∞​(ℝn)→ℝ​[[x]]C^{\infty}(\mathbb{R}^{n})\to\mathbb{R}[\![x]\!] sending a smooth function to its formal Taylor series centred at the origin is surjective by Borel’s lemma [20, §1.5]. so we will sometimes refer to them as formal functions on 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} and denote them by ff or f​(x)f(x) interchangably. The “constant term” c(0,…,0)c_{(0,\ldots,0)} is then denoted by f​(0)f(0); the map f↦f​(0)f\mapsto f(0) is a surjective 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra homomorphism 𝕂​[[x]]→𝕂\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}. We extend this notation to nn-tuples of formal power series φ​(x)=(φ1​(x),…,φn​(x))\varphi(x)=\big(\varphi^{1}(x),\ldots,\varphi^{n}(x)\big) component-wise by setting φ​(0):=(φ1​(0),…,φn​(0))\varphi(0):=\big(\varphi^{1}(0),\ldots,\varphi^{n}(0)\big), and similarly for matrices with entries in 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!].

Since 𝕂\mathbb{K} is a local integral domain, so too is 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]. Its unique maximal ideal 𝔪\mathfrak{m} consists of those power series ff such that f​(0)=0f(0)=0; equivalently, 𝔪\mathfrak{m} is generated by the variables x1,…,xnx^{1},\ldots,x^{n}. We equip 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] with the 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adic topology, which is the translation-invariant topology such that the decreasing filtration by ideals

𝕂​[[x]]=𝔪0⊃𝔪1⊃𝔪2⊃⋯\displaystyle\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]=\mathfrak{m}^{0}\supset\mathfrak{m}^{1}\supset\mathfrak{m}^{2}\supset\cdots

forms a basis of open neighbourhoods of the zero power series. We adopt the Big Theta notation

Θ​(f):=max⁡{k∣f∈𝔪k}=min⁡{|α|∣cα≠0}\displaystyle\Theta(f):=\max\{k\mid f\in\mathfrak{m}^{k}\}=\min\{|\alpha|\mid c_{\alpha}\neq 0\}

to denote the order of a power series f​(x)=∑αcα​xαf(x)=\sum_{\alpha}c_{\alpha}x^{\alpha}, with the convention that the zero power series has order ∞\infty. We extend this notation to nn-tuples of power series φ​(x)=(φ1​(x),…,φn​(x))\varphi(x)=\big(\varphi^{1}(x),\ldots,\varphi^{n}(x)\big) by defining Θ​(φ):=mini⁡Θ​(φi)\Theta(\varphi):=\min_{i}\Theta(\varphi^{i}).

The 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adic topology on 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] is induced by a complete metric; for example, the one given by (f,g)↦2−Θ​(f−g)(f,g)\mapsto 2^{-\Theta(f-g)}. A sequence of power series (fk)(f_{k}) is called Cauchy if it is Cauchy with respect to some (equivalently, every) compatible complete metric. Writing fk​(x)=∑αck,α​xαf_{k}(x)=\sum_{\alpha}c_{k,\alpha}x^{\alpha}, the sequence (fk)(f_{k}) is Cauchy if and only if, for each α\alpha, the sequence of coefficients (ck,α)(c_{k,\alpha}) is eventually constant; the coefficient of xαx^{\alpha} in the limiting power series is then given by limk→∞ck,α\lim_{k\to\infty}c_{k,\alpha}. The infinite sum ∑k∞fk\sum_{k}^{\infty}f_{k} converges if and only if fk→0f_{k}\to 0, or equivalently, Θ​(fk)→∞\Theta(f_{k})\to\infty.

The 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] together with its 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adic topology satisfies a certain universal property, of which we state a special case [26, VII.§1]:

There is a bijective correspondence

{continuous ​𝕂​-algebra endomorphismsφ:𝕂​[[x]]→𝕂​[[x]]}↔{n​-tuples of elements of ​𝔪(φ1​(x),…,φn​(x))}.\displaystyle\left\{\begin{array}[]{c}\text{continuous }\mathbb{K}\text{-algebra endomorphisms}\\ \varphi\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\end{array}\right\}\leftrightarrow\left\{\begin{array}[]{c}n\text{-tuples of elements of }\mathfrak{m}\\ \left(\varphi^{1}(x),\ldots,\varphi^{n}(x)\right)\end{array}\right\}.

One determines the other by defining φ​(xi)=φi​(x)\varphi(x^{i})=\varphi^{i}(x) for each ii.

The power series φi​(x)\varphi^{i}(x) is called the iith component of φ\varphi (or the corresponding nn-tuple). Continuity with respect to the 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adic topology ensures that the image of any power series under φ\varphi may be computed “term-by-term”, even with infinitely many non-zero terms. For example, one always has

∑α∈ℕncα​xα=∑k=0∞(∑|α|=kcα​xα),\displaystyle\sum_{\alpha\in\mathbb{N}^{n}}c_{\alpha}x^{\alpha}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\Bigg(\sum_{|\alpha|=k}c_{\alpha}x^{\alpha}\Bigg), (4)

where the left-hand side is an element of 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] and the right-hand side is a convergent limit of partial sums of a sequence in 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]; continuity allows us to compute

φ​(∑αcα​xα)=∑k=0∞φ​(∑|α|=kcα​xα)=∑αcα​φ​(x1)α1​⋯​φ​(xn)αn.\displaystyle\varphi\bigg(\sum_{\alpha}c_{\alpha}x^{\alpha}\bigg)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\varphi\Bigg(\sum_{|\alpha|=k}c_{\alpha}x^{\alpha}\Bigg)=\sum_{\alpha}c_{\alpha}\varphi(x^{1})^{\alpha_{1}}\cdots\varphi(x^{n})^{\alpha_{n}}.

In this sense, φ\varphi can be thought of as “pullback of formal functions” (but see Remark 2.2).

Remark 2.1.

Actually, for any power series algebras 𝕂​[[x]]=𝕂​[[x1,…,xn]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]=\mathbb{K}[\![x^{1},\ldots,x^{n}]\!] and 𝕂​[[y]]=𝕂​[[y1,…,ym]]\mathbb{K}[\![y]\!]=\mathbb{K}[\![y^{1},\ldots,y^{m}]\!] (possibly in different sets of variables), every 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra homomorphism φ:𝕂​[[x]]→𝕂​[[y]]\varphi\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![y]\!] is automatically continuous with respect to the 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adic topologies, where 𝔪\mathfrak{m} refers to the maximal ideals in both domain and codomain. To see this, recall that a power series with coefficients in any commutative ring RR is invertible if and only if its constant term is invertible in RR. For each i∈{1,…,n}i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}, let ci∈𝕂c^{i}\in\mathbb{K} be the constant term of φ​(xi)\varphi(x^{i}). Since φ\varphi is 𝕂\mathbb{K}-linear and unital, we have

φ​(xi−ci)=φ​(xi)−ci∈𝔪y.\displaystyle\varphi(x^{i}-c^{i})=\varphi(x^{i})-c^{i}\in\mathfrak{m}_{y}.

Thus φ​(xi−ci)\varphi(x^{i}-c^{i}) is not invertible in 𝕂​[[y]]\mathbb{K}[\![y]\!]; as φ\varphi is a unital ring homomorphism, this implies that xi−cix^{i}-c^{i} is not invertible in 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]. Since 𝕂\mathbb{K} is a field, we obtain ci=0c^{i}=0. Thus φ​(xi)∈𝔪y\varphi(x^{i})\in\mathfrak{m}_{y} for each ii; as the xix^{i}’s generate 𝔪x\mathfrak{m}_{x}, we conclude that φ​(𝔪x)⊆𝔪y\varphi(\mathfrak{m}_{x})\subseteq\mathfrak{m}_{y}, so φ\varphi is 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adically continuous.

This argument depends strongly on the assumption that 𝕂\mathbb{K} is a field. Over more general rings, 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adically discontinuous homomorphisms of power series algebras can exist and are more complicated to describe; see [9] and the references within. We explicitly mention continuity in the bijective correspondence above only to emphasize its utility in computations.

A diffeomorphism from a smooth manifold to itself defines an automorphism of its algebra of smooth functions via pullback. In this spirit, we define a formal diffeomorphism222Strictly speaking, what we call a “formal diffeomorphism” more precisely reflects the Taylor series of a smooth diffeomorphism at a fixed point. of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} to be a 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra automorphism of 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]. Thus, a formal diffeomorphism determines a unique nn-tuple of elements of 𝔪\mathfrak{m} via the universal property of 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]. Conversely, given any nn-tuple (φ1​(x),…,φn​(x))\big(\varphi^{1}(x),\ldots,\varphi^{n}(x)\big) of elements of 𝔪\mathfrak{m}, there is a simple criterion to determine when the corresponding endomorphism φ:𝕂​[[x]]→𝕂​[[x]]\varphi\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] is bijective (hence an automorphism). It mimics the inverse function theorem: φ\varphi is an automorphism if and only if the Jacobian matrix of formal partial derivatives

D​φ​(x):=(∂φ1∂x1​(x)…∂φ1∂xn​(x)⋮⋱⋮∂φn∂x1​(x)…∂φn∂xn​(x))\displaystyle D\varphi(x):=\begin{pmatrix}\frac{\partial\varphi^{1}}{\partial x^{1}}(x)&\ldots&\frac{\partial\varphi^{1}}{\partial x^{n}}(x)\\ \vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\ \frac{\partial\varphi^{n}}{\partial x^{1}}(x)&\ldots&\frac{\partial\varphi^{n}}{\partial x^{n}}(x)\end{pmatrix}

is invertible in the ring of n×nn\times n matrices with entries in 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]. As the constant term of detD​φ​(x)\det D\varphi(x) is detD​φ​(0)\det D\varphi(0), this is also equivalent to D​φ​(0)D\varphi(0) being invertible in the ring of n×nn\times n matrices with entries in 𝕂\mathbb{K} [26, VII.§1].

Next, we develop the formal versions of smooth tensor fields. The goal is to mimic smooth expressions at every step; as such, the reader would not be led too far astray by importing the smooth version of each definition/construction and replacing the algebra of smooth functions with the algebra of formal functions everywhere. Nevertheless, we shall describe the main players to eliminate the guesswork needed.

Smooth vector fields act as derivations of a manifold’s algebra of smooth functions. Analogously, we define a formal vector field on 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} to be a derivation of the algebra of formal functions, i.e., a 𝕂\mathbb{K}-linear map X:𝕂​[[x]]→𝕂​[[x]]X\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] satisfying the Leibniz rule:

X​(f​g)=X​(f)​g+f​X​(g),∀f,g∈𝕂​[[x]].\displaystyle X(fg)=X(f)g+fX(g),\qquad\forall f,g\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!].

We denote the set of formal vector fields by 𝔛\mathfrak{X}; it is both a module over 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] and a Lie algebra over 𝕂\mathbb{K} via the commutator Lie bracket [X,Y]:=X∘Y−Y∘X[X,Y]:=X\circ Y-Y\circ X. Every formal vector field is continuous with respect to the 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adic topology; a corollary of this fact is that 𝔛\mathfrak{X} is free of rank nn over 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!], with a basis given by the set of formal partial derivatives {∂∂x1,…,∂∂xn}\left\{\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{1}}},\ldots,\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{n}}}\right\} [4, IV.4.6].

We define a formal covector field on 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} to be an element of the dual 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]-module

Ω1:=Hom𝕂​[[x]]⁡(𝔛,𝕂​[[x]]).\displaystyle\Omega^{1}:=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]}(\mathfrak{X},\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]).

Every formal function f∈𝕂​[[x]]f\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] has a formal differential d​f∈Ω1df\in\Omega^{1}, defined by d​f​(X)=X​(f)df(X)=X(f) for X∈𝔛X\in\mathfrak{X}. The set {d​x1,…,d​xn}\{dx^{1},\ldots,dx^{n}\} forms the dual basis to {∂∂x1,…,∂∂xn}\left\{\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{1}}},\ldots,\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{n}}}\right\}. Thus, every X∈𝔛X\in\mathfrak{X} can be uniquely written in the form X=∑iXi​∂∂xiX=\sum_{i}X^{i}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} where Xi=X​(xi)=d​xi​(X)X^{i}=X(x^{i})=dx^{i}(X), and every ω∈Ω1\omega\in\Omega^{1} can be uniquely written in the form ω=∑iωi​d​xi\omega=\sum_{i}\omega_{i}\,dx^{i} where ωi=ω​(∂∂xi)\omega_{i}=\omega(\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}). In particular, d​f=∑i∂f∂xi​d​xidf=\sum_{i}\frac{\partial f}{\partial x^{i}}\,dx^{i}. (See Remark 2.3 for a comparison of formal covector fields and Kähler differentials.)

Having defined formal vector and covector fields, we define a formal tensor field of type (r,s)(r,s) on 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} to be an element of the tensor product of 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]-modules

𝒯(r,s):=𝔛⊗⋯⊗𝔛⏟r​ times⊗Ω1⊗⋯⊗Ω1⏟s​ times,\displaystyle\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}:=\underbrace{\mathfrak{X}\otimes\cdots\otimes\mathfrak{X}}_{r\text{ times}}\otimes\underbrace{\Omega^{1}\otimes\cdots\otimes\Omega^{1}}_{s\text{ times}},

with the convention that 𝒯(0,0):=𝕂​[[x]]\mathcal{T}^{(0,0)}:=\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]. Since 𝔛\mathfrak{X} and Ω1\Omega^{1} are free of finite rank, formal tensor fields A∈𝒯(r,s)A\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)} are canonically identified with 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]-multilinear maps

A:Ω1×⋯×Ω1⏟r​ times×𝔛×⋯×𝔛⏟s​ times→𝕂​[[x]].\displaystyle A\colon\underbrace{\Omega^{1}\times\cdots\times\Omega^{1}}_{r\text{ times}}\times\underbrace{\mathfrak{X}\times\cdots\times\mathfrak{X}}_{s\text{ times}}\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!].

The tensor product operation ⊗\otimes endows

𝒯:=⨁r,s≥0𝒯(r,s)\displaystyle\mathcal{T}:=\bigoplus_{r,s\geq 0}\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}

with the structure of a graded 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]-algebra. A formal tensor derivation is a 𝕂\mathbb{K}-linear map 𝒟:𝒯→𝒯\mathcal{D}\colon\mathcal{T}\to\mathcal{T} that preserves type, commutes with all contraction homomorphisms 𝒯(r,s)→𝒯(r−1,s−1)\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}\to\mathcal{T}^{(r-1,s-1)} (defined by feeding one of the 𝔛\mathfrak{X} factors to one of the Ω1\Omega^{1} factors), and satisfies

𝒟​(A⊗B)=𝒟​(A)⊗B+A⊗𝒟​(B),∀A,B∈𝒯.\displaystyle\mathcal{D}(A\otimes B)=\mathcal{D}(A)\otimes B+A\otimes\mathcal{D}(B),\qquad\forall A,B\in\mathcal{T}.

Two formal tensor derivations are identical if they agree on formal functions and formal vector fields. Conversely, every pair of 𝕂\mathbb{K}-linear maps 𝒟(0,0):𝕂​[[x]]→𝕂​[[x]]\mathcal{D}^{(0,0)}\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] and 𝒟(1,0):𝔛→𝔛\mathcal{D}^{(1,0)}\colon\mathfrak{X}\to\mathfrak{X} satisfying

𝒟(0,0)​(f​g)=𝒟(0,0)​(f)​g+f​𝒟(0,0)​(g),𝒟(1,0)​(f​X)=𝒟(0,0)​(f)​X+f​𝒟(1,0)​(X)\displaystyle\mathcal{D}^{(0,0)}(fg)=\mathcal{D}^{(0,0)}(f)g+f\mathcal{D}^{(0,0)}(g),\qquad\mathcal{D}^{(1,0)}(fX)=\mathcal{D}^{(0,0)}(f)X+f\mathcal{D}^{(1,0)}(X)

for all f,g∈𝕂​[[x]]f,g\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] and X∈𝔛X\in\mathfrak{X} extends uniquely to a formal tensor derivation. In particular, given X∈𝔛X\in\mathfrak{X}, the formal Lie derivative ℒX:𝒯→𝒯\mathcal{L}_{X}\colon\mathcal{T}\to\mathcal{T} is defined to be the unique formal tensor derivation extending ℒX(0,0)​f:=X​(f)\mathcal{L}_{X}^{(0,0)}f:=X(f) and ℒX(1,0)​Y:=[X,Y]\mathcal{L}_{X}^{(1,0)}Y:=[X,Y]. The map X↦ℒXX\mapsto\mathcal{L}_{X} is 𝕂\mathbb{K}-linear.

Just as diffeomorphisms pull back smooth tensor fields, a formal diffeomorphism φ:𝕂​[[x]]→𝕂​[[x]]\varphi\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] extends to a graded 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra automorphism φ∗:𝒯→𝒯\varphi^{*}\colon\mathcal{T}\to\mathcal{T}, called the pullback, by requiring φ∗\varphi^{*} to distribute across ⊗\otimes and commute with all contractions. That is, for formal functions f∈𝒯(0,0)f\in\mathcal{T}^{(0,0)}, define φ∗​f=φ​(f)\varphi^{*}f=\varphi(f). For formal vector fields X∈𝒯(1,0)X\in\mathcal{T}^{(1,0)}, define φ∗​X∈𝒯(1,0)\varphi^{*}X\in\mathcal{T}^{(1,0)} to be the composition

𝕂​[[x]]→φ−1𝕂​[[x]]→𝑋𝕂​[[x]]→𝜑𝕂​[[x]],\displaystyle\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\xrightarrow{\varphi^{-1}}\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\xrightarrow{X}\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\xrightarrow{\varphi}\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!],

so that (φ∗​X)​(φ∗​f)=φ∗​(X​(f))(\varphi^{*}X)(\varphi^{*}f)=\varphi^{*}\big(X(f)\big) for all f∈𝕂​[[x]]f\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]. For formal covector fields ω∈𝒯(0,1)\omega\in\mathcal{T}^{(0,1)}, define φ∗​ω∈𝒯(0,1)\varphi^{*}\omega\in\mathcal{T}^{(0,1)} to be the composition

𝔛→(φ∗)−1𝔛→𝜔𝕂​[[x]]→𝜑𝕂​[[x]],\displaystyle\mathfrak{X}\xrightarrow{(\varphi^{*})^{-1}}\mathfrak{X}\xrightarrow{\omega}\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\xrightarrow{\varphi}\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!],

so that (φ∗​ω)​(φ∗​X)=φ∗​(ω​(X))(\varphi^{*}\omega)(\varphi^{*}X)=\varphi^{*}\big(\omega(X)\big) for all X∈𝔛X\in\mathfrak{X}. For formal tensor fields of arbitrary type A∈𝒯(r,s)A\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}, define φ∗​A∈𝒯(r,s)\varphi^{*}A\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)} by requiring that

(φ∗​A)​(φ∗​ω1,…,φ∗​ωr,φ∗​X1,…,φ∗​Xs)=φ∗​(A​(ω1,…,ωr,X1,…,Xs))\displaystyle(\varphi^{*}A)(\varphi^{*}\omega_{1},\ldots,\varphi^{*}\omega_{r},\varphi^{*}X_{1},\ldots,\varphi^{*}X_{s})=\varphi^{*}\big(A(\omega_{1},\ldots,\omega_{r},X_{1},\ldots,X_{s})\big)

for all ω1,…,ωr∈Ω1\omega_{1},\ldots,\omega_{r}\in\Omega^{1} and X1,…,Xs∈𝔛X_{1},\ldots,X_{s}\in\mathfrak{X}.

A few comparisons to the analogous definitions in the smooth setting assure us that these formal analogues are sensibly defined. Firstly, φ∗\varphi^{*} restricts to a Lie algebra automorphism 𝔛→𝔛\mathfrak{X}\to\mathfrak{X}. Secondly, pullbacks are compatible with Lie derivatives: for any X∈𝔛X\in\mathfrak{X}, the composition φ∗∘ℒX∘(φ∗)−1\varphi^{*}\circ\mathcal{L}_{X}\circ(\varphi^{*})^{-1} is a formal tensor derivation that agrees with ℒφ∗​X\mathcal{L}_{\varphi^{*}X} on formal functions and formal vector fields, so they must agree on all formal tensor fields; that is,

φ∗​(ℒX​A)=ℒφ∗​X​(φ∗​A),∀A∈𝒯.\displaystyle\varphi^{*}(\mathcal{L}_{X}A)=\mathcal{L}_{\varphi^{*}X}(\varphi^{*}A),\qquad\forall A\in\mathcal{T}. (5)

Lastly, pullbacks look as expected “in coordinates”, which we demonstrate with formal vector fields X=∑iXi​∂∂xiX=\sum_{i}X^{i}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} and formal covector fields ω=∑iωi​d​xi\omega=\sum_{i}\omega_{i}\,dx^{i}. If we denote by (φ∗​X)​(x)∈𝕂​[[x]]n(\varphi^{*}X)(x)\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]^{n} the nn-tuple of components of φ∗​X\varphi^{*}X with respect to the basis {∂∂xi}\{\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}\} and we denote by X​(φ​(x))X(\varphi(x)) the nn-tuple (φ​(X1),…,φ​(Xn))∈𝕂​[[x]]n\big(\varphi(X^{1}),\ldots,\varphi(X^{n})\big)\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]^{n}, then by computing (φ∗​X)​(φ∗​(xi))(\varphi^{*}X)(\varphi^{*}(x^{i})), one finds that

X​(φ​(x))\displaystyle X(\varphi(x)) =D​φ​(x)​[(φ∗​X)​(x)].\displaystyle=D\varphi(x)[(\varphi^{*}X)(x)].

As D​φ​(x)D\varphi(x) is invertible in the ring of n×nn\times n matrices with entries in 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!], this can be rewritten as

(φ∗​X)​(x)=D​φ​(x)−1​[X​(φ​(x))],\displaystyle(\varphi^{*}X)(x)=D\varphi(x)^{-1}[X(\varphi(x))],

mimicking the usual pointwise formula for pullbacks of smooth vector fields along diffeomorphisms. Similarly, if we denote by (φ∗​ω)​(x)∈𝕂​[[x]]n(\varphi^{*}\omega)(x)\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]^{n} the nn-tuple of components of φ∗​ω\varphi^{*}\omega with respect to the basis {d​xi}\{dx^{i}\} and we denote by ω​(φ​(x))\omega(\varphi(x)) the nn-tuple (φ​(ω1),…,φ​(ωn))∈𝕂​[[x]]n\big(\varphi(\omega_{1}),\ldots,\varphi(\omega_{n})\big)\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]^{n}, then by computing (φ∗​ω)​(∂∂xi)(\varphi^{*}\omega)\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}\right), one finds that

(φ∗​ω)​(x)=D​φ​(x)t​[ω​(φ​(x))].\displaystyle(\varphi^{*}\omega)(x)=D\varphi(x)^{t}[\omega(\varphi(x))].
Remark 2.2 (terminology and notation).

The “coordinate” calculations above justify why the map on formal tensor fields φ∗:𝒯→𝒯\varphi^{*}\colon\mathcal{T}\to\mathcal{T} is called “pullback” and denoted with an upper star. But if ψ\psi is another formal diffeomorphism, then so is ψ∘φ\psi\circ\varphi, and the pullback maps satisfy (ψ∘φ)∗=ψ∗∘φ∗(\psi\circ\varphi)^{*}=\psi^{*}\circ\varphi^{*}—in apparent conflict with the usual convention that upper stars denote contravariant actions. We will have little need to consider compositions of formal diffeomorphisms, so this should not pose a serious issue. Nevertheless, this can be reconciled with the usual convention by recognizing that formal diffeomorphisms are defined by how they act on formal functions, not on “formal points”. That is, our notation for pullback is contravariant with respect to maps of the spectrum of 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!].

Remark 2.3 (formal covector fields versus Kähler differentials).

Our definitions of Ω1\Omega^{1} and the formal differential d:𝕂​[[x]]→Ω1d\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\to\Omega^{1} differ from the 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]-module of Kähler differentials Ω𝕂​[[x]]/𝕂\Omega_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]/\mathbb{K}} and the universal derivation δ:𝕂​[[x]]→Ω𝕂​[[x]]/𝕂\delta\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\to\Omega_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]/\mathbb{K}} in a few ways. Firstly, Ω𝕂​[[x]]/𝕂\Omega_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]/\mathbb{K}} is neither projective nor finitely generated as a 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]-module [14, Example 5.5a] whereas our Ω1\Omega^{1} is free of finite rank. Secondly, dd satisfies d​f=∑i∂f∂xi​d​xidf=\sum_{i}\frac{\partial f}{\partial x^{i}}\,dx^{i} for all f∈𝕂​[[x]]f\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] whereas—intuitively speaking—δ\delta is only finitely additive, so one should not expect that δ​f=∑i∂f∂xi​δ​xi\delta f=\sum_{i}\frac{\partial f}{\partial x^{i}}\,\delta x^{i} when ff has infinitely many non-zero coefficients. Thirdly, the pairs (Ω1,d)(\Omega^{1},d) and (Ω𝕂​[[x]]/𝕂,δ)(\Omega_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]/\mathbb{K}},\delta) satisfy different universal properties [14, Example 12.7].

To explain the relationship, recall the universal property of (Ω𝕂​[[x]]/𝕂,δ)(\Omega_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]/\mathbb{K}},\delta): for every 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]-module MM, pre-composition with δ\delta defines an isomorphism of 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]-modules

δ∨:Hom𝕂​[[x]]⁡(Ω𝕂​[[x]]/𝕂,M)→Der𝕂⁡(𝕂​[[x]],M),T↦T∘δ.\displaystyle\delta^{\vee}\colon\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]}(\Omega_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]/\mathbb{K}},M)\to\operatorname{Der}_{\mathbb{K}}(\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!],M),\qquad T\mapsto T\circ\delta.

Taking M=𝕂​[[x]]M=\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!], we see that δ∨\delta^{\vee} identifies the dual 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]-module (Ω𝕂​[[x]]/𝕂)∨(\Omega_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]/\mathbb{K}})^{\vee} with 𝔛\mathfrak{X}, and thus δ∨∨\delta^{\vee\vee} identifies Ω1\Omega^{1} with the double dual (Ω𝕂​[[x]]/𝕂)∨∨(\Omega_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]/\mathbb{K}})^{\vee\vee}. As d∈Der𝕂⁡(𝕂​[[x]],Ω1)d\in\operatorname{Der}_{\mathbb{K}}(\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!],\Omega^{1}), there is a unique T∈Hom𝕂​[[x]]⁡(Ω𝕂​[[x]]/𝕂,Ω1)T\in\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]}(\Omega_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]/\mathbb{K}},\Omega^{1}) such that d=T∘δd=T\circ\delta. If ε:Ω𝕂​[[x]]/𝕂→(Ω𝕂​[[x]]/𝕂)∨∨\varepsilon\colon\Omega_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]/\mathbb{K}}\to(\Omega_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]/\mathbb{K}})^{\vee\vee} denotes the canonical homomorphism, given by ε​(ω)​(χ)=χ​(ω)\varepsilon(\omega)(\chi)=\chi(\omega) for ω∈Ω𝕂​[[x]]/𝕂\omega\in\Omega_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]/\mathbb{K}} and χ∈(Ω𝕂​[[x]]/𝕂)∨\chi\in(\Omega_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]/\mathbb{K}})^{\vee}, then by unravelling definitions one obtains the following commutative diagram:

Thus we see that TT is just the canonical homomorphism up to the identification provided by δ∨∨\delta^{\vee\vee}.

2.2 Formal time dependence

We now generalize the formal objects defined so far by allowing them to depend on another formal variable tt in addition to the xix^{i}’s; the development will closely mirror Section 2.1. To begin, we introduce the notation 𝕂​[[t,x]]:=𝕂​[[t,x1,…,xn]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]:=\mathbb{K}[\![t,x^{1},\ldots,x^{n}]\!] for the 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra of “time-dependent” formal functions, from which all other “time-dependent” formal objects shall be built. Analogously to 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!], it is equipped with the 𝔪t,x\mathfrak{m}_{t,x}-adic topology where 𝔪t,x\mathfrak{m}_{t,x} is the (unique maximal) ideal generated by tt and the xix^{i}’s. Thus, any time-dependent formal function

f​(t,x)=∑k,αck,α​tk​xα\displaystyle f(t,x)=\sum_{k,\alpha}c_{k,\alpha}t^{k}x^{\alpha}

is in particular equal to the convergent limit of partial sums f​(t,x)=∑ktk​fk​(x)f(t,x)=\sum_{k}t^{k}f_{k}(x) where

fk​(x)=∑αck,α​xα∈𝕂​[[x]]​ for each ​k,\displaystyle f_{k}(x)=\sum_{\alpha}c_{k,\alpha}x^{\alpha}\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\text{ for each }k,

and we can interchange such infinite sums with continuous operations on 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!].

We distinguish tt from the xix^{i}’s notationally and psychologically because we will eventually try to “evaluate time-dependent formal objects at a fixed time” by replacing tt with an element of 𝕂\mathbb{K} and applying the field operations of 𝕂\mathbb{K} to make sense of the resulting expressions. The simplest example of this is the (continuous by Remark 2.1) 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]-algebra homomorphism

eval0:𝕂​[[t,x]]→𝕂​[[x]],f​(t,x)↦f​(0,x)=f0​(x).\displaystyle\operatorname{eval}_{0}\colon\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!],\qquad f(t,x)\mapsto f(0,x)=f_{0}(x).

Of course, attempting to substitute non-zero values for tt leads to questions of convergence in the 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adic topology of 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]—for example, one cannot hope to define an “evaluation at time t=1t=1” map on all of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!], as demonstrated by the power series ∑k=0∞tk\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}. Nevertheless, we must initially allow arbitrary power series in tt to obtain an existence and uniqueness theorem for flows of formal vector fields (Theorem 2.8); thus we develop the following story with all concepts based on 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!].

We define a formal isotopy of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} to be a 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra automorphism φ:𝕂​[[t,x]]→𝕂​[[t,x]]\varphi\colon\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] such that φ​(t)=t\varphi(t)=t. To see that this is a good formal analogue of a one-parameter family of diffeomorphisms, let us explicitly characterize formal isotopies similarly to how we characterized formal diffeomorphisms. The universal property of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] produces a bijective correspondence

{(automatically continuous) ​𝕂​-algebraendomorphisms ​φ:𝕂​[[t,x]]→𝕂​[[t,x]]}↔{(n+1)​-tuples ​(φ0​(t,x),φ1​(t,x),…,φn​(t,x))such that ​φi​(0,0)=0​ for each ​i∈{0,1,…,n}},\displaystyle\left\{\begin{array}[]{c}\text{(automatically continuous) }\mathbb{K}\text{-algebra}\\ \text{endomorphisms }\varphi\colon\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\end{array}\right\}\leftrightarrow\left\{\begin{array}[]{c}(n+1)\text{-tuples }\left(\varphi^{0}(t,x),\varphi^{1}(t,x),\ldots,\varphi^{n}(t,x)\right)\\ \text{such that }\varphi^{i}(0,0)=0\text{ for each }i\in\{0,1,\ldots,n\}\end{array}\right\},

one determining the other via φ​(t)=φ0\varphi(t)=\varphi^{0} and φ​(xi)=φi\varphi(x^{i})=\varphi^{i} for i∈{1,…,n}i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}. This restricts to a bijective correspondence between 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra automorphisms of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] and such (n+1)(n+1)-tuples satisfying the additional condition that the (n+1)×(n+1)(n+1)\times(n+1) Jacobian matrix

(∂φ0∂t​(t,x)∂φ0∂x1​(t,x)⋯∂φ0∂xn​(t,x)∂φ1∂t​(t,x)∂φ1∂x1​(t,x)⋯∂φ1∂xn​(t,x)⋮⋮⋱⋮∂φn∂t​(t,x)∂φn∂x1​(t,x)…∂φn∂xn​(t,x))\displaystyle\begin{pmatrix}\frac{\partial\varphi^{0}}{\partial t}(t,x)&\frac{\partial\varphi^{0}}{\partial x^{1}}(t,x)&\cdots&\frac{\partial\varphi^{0}}{\partial x^{n}}(t,x)\\ \frac{\partial\varphi^{1}}{\partial t}(t,x)&\frac{\partial\varphi^{1}}{\partial x^{1}}(t,x)&\cdots&\frac{\partial\varphi^{1}}{\partial x^{n}}(t,x)\\ \vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\ \frac{\partial\varphi^{n}}{\partial t}(t,x)&\frac{\partial\varphi^{n}}{\partial x^{1}}(t,x)&\ldots&\frac{\partial\varphi^{n}}{\partial x^{n}}(t,x)\end{pmatrix}

is invertible. For (n+1)(n+1)-tuples with φ0​(t,x)=t\varphi^{0}(t,x)=t (so their corresponding endomorphisms fix tt), the top row of the above matrix is (10⋯0)\begin{pmatrix}1&0&\cdots&0\end{pmatrix}, so this matrix is invertible if and only if the bottom-right n×nn\times n submatrix

Dx​φ​(t,x):=(∂φ1∂x1​(t,x)⋯∂φ1∂xn​(t,x)⋮⋱⋮∂φn∂x1​(t,x)…∂φn∂xn​(t,x))\displaystyle D_{x}\varphi(t,x):=\begin{pmatrix}\frac{\partial\varphi^{1}}{\partial x^{1}}(t,x)&\cdots&\frac{\partial\varphi^{1}}{\partial x^{n}}(t,x)\\ \vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\ \frac{\partial\varphi^{n}}{\partial x^{1}}(t,x)&\ldots&\frac{\partial\varphi^{n}}{\partial x^{n}}(t,x)\end{pmatrix}

is invertible in the ring of n×nn\times n matrices with entries in 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!], or equivalently, Dx​φ​(0,0)D_{x}\varphi(0,0) is invertible in the ring of n×nn\times n matrices with entries in 𝕂\mathbb{K}. Knowing that φ0​(t,x)=t\varphi^{0}(t,x)=t, we might as well omit it from such (n+1)(n+1)-tuples; thus, we obtain a bijective correspondence

{formal isotopiesφ:𝕂​[[t,x]]→𝕂​[[t,x]]}↔{n​-tuples ​φ​(t,x)∈𝕂​[[t,x]]n​ such thatφ​(0,0)=0​ and ​Dx​φ​(0,0)​ is invertible}.\displaystyle\left\{\begin{array}[]{c}\text{formal isotopies}\\ \varphi\colon\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\end{array}\right\}\leftrightarrow\left\{\begin{array}[]{c}n\text{-tuples }\varphi(t,x)\in\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]^{n}\text{ such that}\\ \varphi(0,0)=0\text{ and }D_{x}\varphi(0,0)\text{ is invertible}\end{array}\right\}.

Compare this with how formal diffeomorphisms correspond to nn-tuples in 𝕂​[[x]]n\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]^{n}: here, the nn component power series are allowed to depend on tt but the relevant Jacobian matrix still involves partial derivatives only with respect to the xix^{i}’s. We will typically decorate 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra endomorphisms of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] with a subscript tt (as in φt\varphi_{t}) to emphasize the allowed dependence on tt.

Remark 2.4.

Any nn-tuple φ​(t,x)∈𝕂​[[t,x]]n\varphi(t,x)\in\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]^{n} can be written in the form φ​(t,x)=∑k=0∞tk​φk​(x)\varphi(t,x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}\varphi_{k}(x) where φk​(x)∈𝕂​[[x]]n\varphi_{k}(x)\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]^{n}; written this way, φ​(t,x)\varphi(t,x) corresponds to a formal isotopy if and only if Θ​(φ0)≥1\Theta(\varphi_{0})\geq 1 and D​φ0​(0)D\varphi_{0}(0) is invertible.

Every 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra endomorphism of 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] (resp. formal diffeomorphism) extends to a “time-independent” 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra endomorphism of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] (resp. formal isotopy) by declaring it to fix tt. On the level of nn-tuples, this is simply the inclusion 𝕂​[[x]]n⊂𝕂​[[t,x]]n\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]^{n}\subset\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]^{n}. Conversely, we define the t=0t=0 time slice of any 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra endomorphism φt\varphi_{t} of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] that fixes tt by the composition

φ0:𝕂​[[x]]↪𝕂​[[t,x]]→φt𝕂​[[t,x]]→eval0𝕂​[[x]].\displaystyle\varphi_{0}\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\hookrightarrow\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\xrightarrow{\varphi_{t}}\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\xrightarrow{\operatorname{eval}_{0}}\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!].

The subscript 0 notation is consistent with identifying φt\varphi_{t} with an nn-tuple written as in Remark 2.4, as φ0\varphi_{0} corresponds to the nn-tuple φ0​(x)∈𝕂​[[x]]n\varphi_{0}(x)\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]^{n}. Time zero slices are compatible with composition:

Proposition 2.5.

(ψt∘φt)0=ψ0∘φ0(\psi_{t}\circ\varphi_{t})_{0}=\psi_{0}\circ\varphi_{0} for all 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra endomorphisms φt,ψt\varphi_{t},\psi_{t} of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] fixing tt. In particular, if φt\varphi_{t} is a formal isotopy, then φ0\varphi_{0} is a formal diffeomorphism and (φ0)−1=(φt−1)0(\varphi_{0})^{-1}=(\varphi_{t}^{-1})_{0}.

Proof 2.6.

Denote the inclusion by ı:𝕂​[[x]]↪𝕂​[[t,x]]\imath\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\hookrightarrow\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]. As eval0∘ı\operatorname{eval}_{0}\circ\,\imath is the identity map on 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!], we have

eval0∘φt∘ı=φ0=eval0∘ı∘φ0.\displaystyle\operatorname{eval}_{0}\circ\,\varphi_{t}\circ\imath=\varphi_{0}=\operatorname{eval}_{0}\circ\,\imath\circ\varphi_{0}.

Thus, the image of φt∘ı−ı∘φ0\varphi_{t}\circ\imath-\imath\circ\varphi_{0} is contained in the kernel of eval0\operatorname{eval}_{0}. Since ψt\psi_{t} is continuous and fixes tt, it maps ker⁡(eval0)\ker(\operatorname{eval}_{0}) to itself, so for all f∈𝕂​[[x]]f\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!],

(ψt∘φt)0​(f)−(ψ0∘φ0)​(f)\displaystyle(\psi_{t}\circ\varphi_{t})_{0}(f)-(\psi_{0}\circ\varphi_{0})(f) =eval0⁡[ψt​((φt∘ı)​(f)−(ı∘φ0)​(f))]=0.\displaystyle=\operatorname{eval}_{0}\left[\psi_{t}\Big((\varphi_{t}\circ\imath)(f)-(\imath\circ\varphi_{0})(f)\Big)\right]=0.

Thus (ψt∘φt)0=ψ0∘φ0(\psi_{t}\circ\varphi_{t})_{0}=\psi_{0}\circ\varphi_{0}.

Remark 2.7.

Given an algebra AA, algebra homomorphisms A→A​[[t]]A\to A[\![t]\!] are sometimes called Hasse–Schmidt derivations of AA in the literature [11]. Thus, any algebra endomorphism of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] can be regarded as a Hasse–Schmidt derivation of 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] by restricting its domain, and any Hasse–Schmidt derivation of 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] extends to an algebra endomorphism of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] by declaring it to fix tt. We prefer to avoid this terminology, as the results to come are more naturally expressed in terms of endomorphisms of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] than in terms of homomorphisms 𝕂​[[x]]→𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!].

Before defining time-dependent formal tensor fields on 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n}, we note that by treating tt as “just another formal variable” no different from the xix^{i}’s, one obtains formal tensor fields on 𝕂n+1\mathbb{K}^{n+1}: the 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]-module of derivations of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!], its dual, tensor products of these, and the associated definitions of Lie derivatives and pullbacks (along formal diffeomorphisms of 𝕂n+1\mathbb{K}^{n+1}). Morally speaking, time-dependent formal tensor fields on 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} are simply formal tensor fields on 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n}, except that when written in terms of tensor products of ∂∂xi\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}’s and d​xidx^{i}’s, their component power series are also allowed to depend on tt. Equivalently, they are formal tensor fields on 𝕂n+1\mathbb{K}^{n+1} that do not involve ∂∂t\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}} or d​tdt.

A “coordinate-free” way to implement this is by declaring a time-dependent formal tensor field of type (r,s)(r,s) on 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} to be an element of the 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]-module obtained from the 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]-module 𝒯(r,s)\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)} by extension of scalars along the inclusion ı:𝕂​[[x]]↪𝕂​[[t,x]]\imath\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\hookrightarrow\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]:

𝒯t(r,s):=𝕂​[[t,x]]⊗𝕂​[[x]]𝒯(r,s).\displaystyle\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_{t}:=\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\otimes_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]}\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}.

We denote by 𝔛t:=𝒯t(1,0)\mathfrak{X}_{t}:=\mathcal{T}^{(1,0)}_{t} and Ωt1:=𝒯t(0,1)\Omega^{1}_{t}:=\mathcal{T}^{(0,1)}_{t} the time-dependent formal vector and covector fields, respectively, which are free 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]-modules with bases {∂∂x1,…,∂∂xn}\left\{\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{1}}},\ldots,\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{n}}}\right\} and {d​x1,…,d​xn}\{dx^{1},\ldots,dx^{n}\} by construction. In particular, 𝔛t\mathfrak{X}_{t} is identified with the set of derivations of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] that vanish on tt, which is a 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]-submodule and Lie subalgebra of the set of all derivations of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]. Since 𝒯(r,s)\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)} is free of finite rank over 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!], the definition of 𝒯t(r,s)\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_{t} is compatible with thinking of it as (𝔛t)⊗r⊗(Ωt1)⊗s(\mathfrak{X}_{t})^{\otimes r}\otimes(\Omega^{1}_{t})^{\otimes s} (the tensor products being taken over 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]), or as 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]-multilinear maps

Ωt1×⋯×Ωt1⏟r​ times×𝔛t×⋯×𝔛t⏟s​ times→𝕂​[[t,x]].\displaystyle\underbrace{\Omega^{1}_{t}\times\cdots\times\Omega^{1}_{t}}_{r\text{ times}}\times\underbrace{\mathfrak{X}_{t}\times\cdots\times\mathfrak{X}_{t}}_{s\text{ times}}\to\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!].

As with formal isotopies, time-dependent formal tensor fields may be decorated with a subscript tt (XtX_{t}, ωt\omega_{t}, etc.) to serve as a reminder that their components are power series in both tt and the xix^{i}’s.

By tensoring with the identity maps 𝒯(r,s)→𝒯(r,s)\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}\to\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}, the inclusion ı:𝕂​[[x]]↪𝕂​[[t,x]]\imath\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\hookrightarrow\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] induces inclusions ı⊗id:𝒯(r,s)↪𝒯t(r,s)\imath\otimes\operatorname{id}\colon\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}\hookrightarrow\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_{t} of “time-independent” formal tensor fields into time-dependent formal tensor fields. For (r,s)=(1,0)(r,s)=(1,0), this simply means that every derivation 𝕂​[[x]]→𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] can be extended to a derivation 𝕂​[[t,x]]→𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] by declaring it to vanish on tt. For (r,s)=(0,1)(r,s)=(0,1), this means that every 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]-linear map 𝔛→𝕂​[[x]]\mathfrak{X}\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] can be post-composed with ı\imath to obtain an element of Hom𝕂​[[x]]⁡(𝔛,𝕂​[[t,x]])≅Hom𝕂​[[t,x]]⁡(𝔛t,𝕂​[[t,x]])≅Ωt1\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]}(\mathfrak{X},\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!])\cong\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]}(\mathfrak{X}_{t},\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!])\cong\Omega^{1}_{t} (using the universal property of extension by scalars). In a basis, this inclusion means: write out any A∈𝒯(r,s)A\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)} in terms of tensor products of ∂∂xi\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}’s and d​xidx^{i}’s, and simply regard each component as a power series in 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] instead of 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!].

Conversely, the image of a time-dependent formal tensor field At∈𝒯t(r,s)A_{t}\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_{t} under the projection eval0⊗id:𝒯t(r,s)→𝒯(r,s)\operatorname{eval}_{0}\otimes\operatorname{id}\colon\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_{t}\to\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)} is called its t=0t=0 time slice and denoted by A0A_{0}. Regarding AtA_{t} as a 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]-multilinear map

At:Ωt1×⋯×Ωt1⏟r​ times×𝔛t×⋯×𝔛t⏟s​ times→𝕂​[[t,x]],\displaystyle A_{t}\colon\underbrace{\Omega^{1}_{t}\times\cdots\times\Omega^{1}_{t}}_{r\text{ times}}\times\underbrace{\mathfrak{X}_{t}\times\cdots\times\mathfrak{X}_{t}}_{s\text{ times}}\to\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!],

this corresponds to pre-composing each factor in the domain with the inclusions 𝔛↪𝔛t\mathfrak{X}\hookrightarrow\mathfrak{X}_{t} and Ω1↪Ωt1\Omega^{1}\hookrightarrow\Omega^{1}_{t} described above, then post-composing with eval0:𝕂​[[t,x]]→𝕂​[[x]]\operatorname{eval}_{0}\colon\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] to obtain the 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]-multilinear map

A0:Ω1×⋯×Ω1⏟r​ times×𝔛×⋯×𝔛⏟s​ times→𝕂​[[x]].\displaystyle A_{0}\colon\underbrace{\Omega^{1}\times\cdots\times\Omega^{1}}_{r\text{ times}}\times\underbrace{\mathfrak{X}\times\cdots\times\mathfrak{X}}_{s\text{ times}}\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!].

Equivalently, write out AtA_{t} in the 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]-basis of tensor products of ∂∂xi\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}’s and d​xidx^{i}’s, then set t=0t=0 in each of its component power series; this is A0∈𝒯(r,s)A_{0}\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}.

As mentioned above, 𝔛t\mathfrak{X}_{t} is a Lie subalgebra of the set of all derivations of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]. In fact, more is true: for every Xt∈𝔛tX_{t}\in\mathfrak{X}_{t}, the Lie bracket

d​Xtd​t:=[∂∂t,Xt]\displaystyle\frac{dX_{t}}{dt}:=\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}},X_{t}\right]

is still in 𝔛t\mathfrak{X}_{t} (even though ∂∂t∉𝔛t\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\notin\mathfrak{X}_{t}). It follows that the formal Lie derivative ℒ∂∂t\mathcal{L}_{\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}}, thought of as a formal tensor derivation in one dimension higher, preserves the set of time-dependent formal tensor fields on 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n}. Denoting the restriction of ℒ∂∂t\mathcal{L}_{\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}} to 𝒯t(r,s)\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_{t} by dd​t\frac{d}{dt}, we obtain a short exact sequence

0→𝒯(r,s)↪ı⊗id𝒯t(r,s)→dd​t𝒯t(r,s)→0\displaystyle 0\to\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}\xhookrightarrow{\imath\otimes\operatorname{id}}\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_{t}\xrightarrow{\frac{d}{dt}}\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_{t}\to 0

of 𝕂\mathbb{K}-vector spaces. Simply put, At∈𝒯t(r,s)A_{t}\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_{t} is time-independent if and only if its “time derivative” d​Atd​t\frac{dA_{t}}{dt} vanishes, if and only if all of the component power series of AtA_{t} are time-independent. When this is the case, we may identify AtA_{t} with its t=0t=0 time slice.

Suppose φt:𝕂​[[t,x]]→𝕂​[[t,x]]\varphi_{t}\colon\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] is a formal isotopy of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n}. Thought of as a formal diffeomorphism of 𝕂n+1\mathbb{K}^{n+1}, the pullback φt∗\varphi_{t}^{*} of formal tensor fields on 𝕂n+1\mathbb{K}^{n+1} preserves the set of time-dependent formal tensor fields on 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n}. For example, regarding Xt∈𝔛tX_{t}\in\mathfrak{X}_{t} as a derivation of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!], we have

(φt∗​Xt)​(t)=φt∗​(Xt​(t))=0,\displaystyle(\varphi_{t}^{*}X_{t})(t)=\varphi_{t}^{*}\big(X_{t}(t)\big)=0,

so φt∗​Xt∈𝔛t\varphi_{t}^{*}X_{t}\in\mathfrak{X}_{t}. Given At∈𝒯t(r,s)A_{t}\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_{t}, let us temporarily denote the t=0t=0 time slice of the pullback φt∗​At\varphi_{t}^{*}A_{t} by (φt∗​At)|t=0(\varphi_{t}^{*}A_{t})|_{t=0}. We can compare this to the pullback, in the sense defined in Section 2.1, of the time-independent formal tensor field A0A_{0} along the formal diffeomorphism φ0\varphi_{0} of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} (Proposition 2.5). It should not be too surprising that these coincide:

(φt∗​At)|t=0=φ0∗​A0.\displaystyle(\varphi_{t}^{*}A_{t})|_{t=0}=\varphi_{0}^{*}A_{0}. (6)

Section 2.4 will be dedicated to formulating definitions to make sense of this equality for non-zero values of tt, provided that AtA_{t} and φt\varphi_{t} satisfy certain conditions (Proposition 2.22).

2.3 Integration of formal vector fields

Just as smooth vector fields integrate to flows, so too do their formal versions. We acknowledge that a formal power series version of existence and uniqueness of solutions to initial value ODE problems is neither new to state nor difficult to prove: one can of course regard a formal vector field as an nn-tuple of power series (X1,…,Xn)(X^{1},\ldots,X^{n}) and manually find a power series solution to d​xid​t=Xi​(x​(t))\frac{dx^{i}}{dt}=X^{i}(x(t)) by comparing coefficients on each side. However, we were unable to locate a formulation of this theorem that regards formal flows by how they act on formal functions, and the resulting consequences for how they act on formal tensor fields. Moreover, formal flows of time-dependent vector fields seem less commonly discussed, but will be essential for our formal version of Moser’s trick. As such, we supply a statement and proof.

Theorem 2.8.

For every Xt∈𝔛tX_{t}\in\mathfrak{X}_{t}, there exists a unique formal isotopy φt\varphi_{t} of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n}, called the flow of XtX_{t}, satisfying the following two properties:

  1. (IC)

    Initial condition: the t=0t=0 time slice φ0:𝕂​[[x]]→𝕂​[[x]]\varphi_{0}\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] is the identity map.

  2. (FP)

    Flow property: for all f∈𝕂​[[x]]f\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!], we have

    ∂∂t​(φt​(f))=φt​(Xt​(f)).\displaystyle\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\varphi_{t}(f)\big)=\varphi_{t}\big(X_{t}(f)\big).
Remark 2.9.

Conversely, every formal isotopy φt\varphi_{t} such that φ0=id𝕂​[[x]]\varphi_{0}=\operatorname{id}_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]} is the flow of a unique time-dependent formal vector field Xt∈𝔛tX_{t}\in\mathfrak{X}_{t}, but this is easy to see: the formula

Xt:𝕂​[[t,x]]→𝕂​[[t,x]],Xt​(f):=φt−1​(∂∂t​(φt​(f)))−∂f∂t\displaystyle X_{t}\colon\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!],\qquad X_{t}(f):=\varphi_{t}^{-1}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\varphi_{t}(f)\big)\right)-\frac{\partial f}{\partial t}

defines a derivation of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] such that Xt​(t)=0X_{t}(t)=0, and the flow property holds for all f∈𝕂​[[x]]f\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] by construction. (The significance of the extra −∂f∂t-\frac{\partial f}{\partial t} term will become apparent from Proposition 2.11.)

Proof 2.10.

Step 1: uniqueness. Suppose φt\varphi_{t} and ψt\psi_{t} are formal isotopies that both satisfy the initial condition (IC) and the flow property (FP). It suffices to show that φt​(xi)=ψt​(xi)\varphi_{t}(x^{i})=\psi_{t}(x^{i}) for each i∈{1,…,n}i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}; this means that φt\varphi_{t} and ψt\psi_{t} correspond to the same nn-tuple, hence must be equal. By way of contradiction, suppose that there is some ii for which Δi​(t,x):=φt​(xi)−ψt​(xi)\Delta^{i}(t,x):=\varphi_{t}(x^{i})-\psi_{t}(x^{i}) is non-zero. Then there is a largest integer mm such that every Δi\Delta^{i} is divisible by tmt^{m}. In fact, by the initial condition we have Δi​(0,x)=φ0​(xi)−ψ0​(xi)=0\Delta^{i}(0,x)=\varphi_{0}(x^{i})-\psi_{0}(x^{i})=0, so m≥1m\geq 1.

We claim that φt​(f)−ψt​(f)\varphi_{t}(f)-\psi_{t}(f) is divisible by tmt^{m} for all f∈𝕂​[[t,x]]f\in\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]. This is true for constants f​(t,x)=c∈𝕂f(t,x)=c\in\mathbb{K} as φt\varphi_{t} and ψt\psi_{t} are 𝕂\mathbb{K}-linear and unital; it is true for f​(t,x)=tf(t,x)=t as φt​(t)=ψt​(t)=t\varphi_{t}(t)=\psi_{t}(t)=t; it is true for f​(t,x)=xif(t,x)=x^{i} by assumption; if it is true for ff and gg, then in view of the identity

φt​(f​g)−ψt​(f​g)=φt​(f)​[φt​(g)−ψt​(g)]+ψt​(g)​[φt​(f)−ψt​(f)],\displaystyle\varphi_{t}(fg)-\psi_{t}(fg)=\varphi_{t}(f)\big[\varphi_{t}(g)-\psi_{t}(g)\big]+\psi_{t}(g)\big[\varphi_{t}(f)-\psi_{t}(f)\big],

it is also true for f​gfg. Thus the claim holds for all monomials tk​xαt^{k}x^{\alpha} by induction; by linearity and continuity, it holds for all f∈𝕂​[[t,x]]f\in\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!].

The flow property yields

∂Δi∂t=∂∂t​(φt​(xi))−∂∂t​(ψt​(xi))=φt​(Xt​(xi))−ψt​(Xt​(xi)),\displaystyle\frac{\partial\Delta^{i}}{\partial t}=\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\varphi_{t}(x^{i})\big)-\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\psi_{t}(x^{i})\big)=\varphi_{t}\big(X_{t}(x^{i})\big)-\psi_{t}\big(X_{t}(x^{i})\big),

so each ∂Δi∂t\frac{\partial\Delta^{i}}{\partial t} is divisible by tmt^{m} by what we have just proved. As m≥1m\geq 1, this implies that the Δi\Delta^{i}’s are all in fact divisible by tm+1t^{m+1}, in contradiction with mm being the largest such integer.

Step 2: existence for time-independent vector fields XX. We claim that the flow of XX is given by the formal exponential formula

et​X:𝕂​[[t,x]]→𝕂​[[t,x]],et​X​(f):=∑k=0∞tkk!​X∘k​(f),∀f∈𝕂​[[t,x]].\displaystyle e^{tX}\colon\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!],\qquad e^{tX}(f):=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{t^{k}}{k!}X^{\circ k}(f),\qquad\forall f\in\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!].

Here, X∘k:=X∘⋯∘XX^{\circ k}:=X\circ\cdots\circ X denotes the kk-fold iteration of XX (the identity for k=0k=0). Observe that:

  • •

    k!k! is invertible as 𝕂\mathbb{K} is a field of characteristic zero (so each term in the infinite sum is well-defined) and tk​X∘k​(f)∈𝔪t,xkt^{k}X^{\circ k}(f)\in\mathfrak{m}_{t,x}^{k} (so the sum converges in the 𝔪t,x\mathfrak{m}_{t,x}-adic topology).

  • •

    et​Xe^{tX} is 𝕂\mathbb{K}-linear as each X∘kX^{\circ k} is 𝕂\mathbb{K}-linear; it is clearly unital; the higher-order Leibniz rule X∘k​(f​g)=∑i=0k(ki)​X∘i​(f)​X∘(k−i)​(g)X^{\circ k}(fg)=\sum_{i=0}^{k}\binom{k}{i}X^{\circ i}(f)X^{\circ(k-i)}(g) implies that

    et​X​(f​g)\displaystyle e^{tX}(fg) =∑k=0∞∑i=0ktki!​(k−i)!​X∘i​(f)​X∘(k−i)​(g)\displaystyle=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\sum_{i=0}^{k}\frac{t^{k}}{i!(k-i)!}X^{\circ i}(f)X^{\circ(k-i)}(g)
    =∑k=0∞∑i,j≥0i+j=kti+ji!​j!​X∘i​(f)​X∘j​(g)\displaystyle=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}i,j\geq 0\\ i+j=k\end{subarray}}\frac{t^{i+j}}{i!j!}X^{\circ i}(f)X^{\circ j}(g)
    =(∑i=0∞tii!​X∘i​(f))​(∑j=0∞tjj!​X∘j​(g))\displaystyle=\left(\sum_{i=0}^{\infty}\frac{t^{i}}{i!}X^{\circ i}(f)\right)\left(\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}\frac{t^{j}}{j!}X^{\circ j}(g)\right)
    =et​X​(f)​et​X​(g),\displaystyle=e^{tX}(f)e^{tX}(g),

    so et​Xe^{tX} is a 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra homomorphism.

  • •

    Using the identity ∑i=0k(−1)ii!​(k−i)!=0\sum_{i=0}^{k}\frac{(-1)^{i}}{i!(k-i)!}=0 for k>0k>0, we compute

    et​(−X)​(et​X​(f))\displaystyle e^{t(-X)}\big(e^{tX}(f)\big) =∑i=0∞∑j=0∞ti+ji!​j!​(−1)i​X∘(i+j)​(f)=∑k=0∞tk​X∘k​(f)​∑i=0k(−1)ii!​(k−i)!=f.\displaystyle=\sum_{i=0}^{\infty}\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}\frac{t^{i+j}}{i!j!}(-1)^{i}X^{\circ(i+j)}(f)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}X^{\circ k}(f)\sum_{i=0}^{k}\frac{(-1)^{i}}{i!(k-i)!}=f.

    Reversing the roles of XX and −X-X, we see that et​Xe^{tX} is inverted by et​(−X)e^{t(-X)}.

  • •

    Since X∘k​(t)=0X^{\circ k}(t)=0 for all k>0k>0, we have et​X​(t)=te^{tX}(t)=t, so et​Xe^{tX} is a formal isotopy of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n}.

  • •

    Setting t=0t=0 in the exponential formula immediately shows that et​Xe^{tX} satisfies the initial condition (IC). For the flow property (FP), fix f∈𝕂​[[x]]f\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]. Continuity of ∂∂t\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}} yields

    ∂∂t​(et​X​(f))\displaystyle\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(e^{tX}(f)\big) =∑k=0∞∂∂t​(tkk!​X∘k​(f))=∑k=0∞kk!​tk−1​X∘k​(f)+∑k=0∞tkk!​∂∂t​(X∘k​(f)).\displaystyle=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\left(\frac{t^{k}}{k!}X^{\circ k}(f)\right)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{k}{k!}t^{k-1}X^{\circ k}(f)+\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{t^{k}}{k!}\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(X^{\circ k}(f)\big).

    The second sum on the right-hand side vanishes since XX and ff are time-independent: [∂∂t,X]=0\big[\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}},X\big]=0, hence ∂∂t​(X∘k​(f))=X∘k​(∂f∂t)=0\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(X^{\circ k}(f)\big)=X^{\circ k}\big(\frac{\partial f}{\partial t}\big)=0. Re-indexing the first sum then yields

    ∂∂t​(et​X​(f))\displaystyle\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(e^{tX}(f)\big) =∑k=0∞tkk!​X∘(k+1)​(f)=et​X​(X​(f)).\displaystyle=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{t^{k}}{k!}X^{\circ(k+1)}(f)=e^{tX}\big(X(f)\big).

Step 3: existence for time-dependent vector fields XtX_{t}. We mimic the standard procedure for converting a non-autonomous system of nn ordinary differential equations into an autonomous system of n+1n+1 equations. That is, temporarily introduce an additional formal variable ss and let XsX_{s} denote the same vector field as XtX_{t}, but with every instance of tt replaced with ss. Then X~:=∂∂s+Xs\widetilde{X}:=\frac{\partial}{\partial{s}}+X_{s} is a derivation of 𝕂​[[s,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![s,x]\!]—a time-independent formal vector field on 𝕂n+1\mathbb{K}^{n+1}—which has a unique flow Φt:𝕂​[[t,s,x]]→𝕂​[[t,s,x]]\Phi_{t}\colon\mathbb{K}[\![t,s,x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![t,s,x]\!] by the previous steps, given by the formal exponential et​X~e^{t\widetilde{X}}. We claim that the desired flow of XtX_{t} is given by the composition

φt:𝕂​[[t,x]]↪𝕂​[[t,s,x]]→Φt𝕂​[[t,s,x]]→set ​s=0𝕂​[[t,x]].\displaystyle\varphi_{t}\colon\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\hookrightarrow\mathbb{K}[\![t,s,x]\!]\xrightarrow{\Phi_{t}}\mathbb{K}[\![t,s,x]\!]\xrightarrow{\text{set }s=0}\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!].

Each map in this composition is a 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra homomorphism that fixes tt, so the same is true of φt\varphi_{t}. To see that φt\varphi_{t} is bijective (hence a formal isotopy), observe that its iith component is

Φt​(xi)|s=0=xi+t​X~​(xi)|s=0+t22!​X~∘2​(xi)|s=0+⋯\displaystyle\Phi_{t}(x^{i})\big|_{s=0}=x^{i}+t\widetilde{X}(x^{i})\big|_{s=0}+\frac{t^{2}}{2!}\widetilde{X}^{\circ 2}(x^{i})\big|_{s=0}+\cdots

by the exponential formula, from which it follows that Dx​φ​(0,0)D_{x}\varphi(0,0) is the identity matrix.

To show that φt\varphi_{t} satisfies the initial condition (IC), observe that φ0\varphi_{0} is given by following the top arrows in the diagram below; it is equal to the identity on 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] because the diagram commutes:

(The bottom trapezoid commuting amounts to the fact that Φt\Phi_{t} satisfies (IC) on 𝕂​[[s,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![s,x]\!].)

Observe that if the flow property (FP) holds for ff and gg, then it also holds for their product:

∂∂t​(φt​(f​g))=∂∂t​(φt​(f))​φt​(g)+φt​(f)​∂∂t​(φt​(g))=φt​(Xt​(f)​g+f​Xt​(g))=φt​(Xt​(f​g)).\displaystyle\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\varphi_{t}(fg)\big)=\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\varphi_{t}(f)\big)\varphi_{t}(g)+\varphi_{t}(f)\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\varphi_{t}(g)\big)=\varphi_{t}\big(X_{t}(f)g+fX_{t}(g)\big)=\varphi_{t}\big(X_{t}(fg)\big).

Thus, it suffices to prove that φt\varphi_{t} satisfies the flow property for f​(t,x)=xif(t,x)=x^{i} (i∈{1,…,n}i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}); then by induction it holds for all monomials xαx^{\alpha}; by linearity and continuity, it therefore holds for all f∈𝕂​[[x]]f\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]. To that end, first note that X~​(s)=1\widetilde{X}(s)=1 and X~∘k​(s)=0\widetilde{X}^{\circ k}(s)=0 for k>1k>1, so

Φt​(s)\displaystyle\Phi_{t}(s) =∑k=0∞tkk!​X~∘k​(s)=s+t.\displaystyle=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{t^{k}}{k!}\widetilde{X}^{\circ k}(s)=s+t.

For each ii, the power series X~​(xi)−Xt​(xi)\widetilde{X}(x^{i})-X_{t}(x^{i}) is divisible by s−ts-t as its terms can be grouped into pairs that all have a factor of sk−tk=(s−t)​(sk−1+sk−2​t+⋯+s​tk−2+tk−1)s^{k}-t^{k}=(s-t)(s^{k-1}+s^{k-2}t+\cdots+st^{k-2}+t^{k-1}). Thus we may write X~​(xi)−Xt​(xi)=(s−t)​fi\widetilde{X}(x^{i})-X_{t}(x^{i})=(s-t)f^{i} for some fi∈𝕂​[[t,s,x]]f^{i}\in\mathbb{K}[\![t,s,x]\!]. It follows that

Φt​(X~​(xi)−Xt​(xi))=Φt​(s−t)​Φt​(fi)=s​Φt​(fi),\displaystyle\Phi_{t}\big(\widetilde{X}(x^{i})-X_{t}(x^{i})\big)=\Phi_{t}(s-t)\Phi_{t}(f^{i})=s\Phi_{t}(f^{i}),

so upon setting s=0s=0 we obtain

Φt​(X~​(xi))|s=0=Φt​(Xt​(xi))|s=0=φt​(Xt​(xi)).\displaystyle\Phi_{t}\big(\widetilde{X}(x^{i})\big)\big|_{s=0}=\Phi_{t}\big(X_{t}(x^{i})\big)\big|_{s=0}=\varphi_{t}\big(X_{t}(x^{i})\big).

Since ∂∂t\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}} commutes with setting s=0s=0 and Φt\Phi_{t} satisfies the flow property on 𝕂​[[s,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![s,x]\!] (but with the vector field X~\widetilde{X}), it follows that

∂∂t​(φt​(xi))\displaystyle\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\varphi_{t}(x^{i})\big) =∂∂t​(Φt​(xi)|s=0)\displaystyle=\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\left(\Phi_{t}(x^{i})\big|_{s=0}\right)
=[∂∂t​(Φt​(xi))]|s=0\displaystyle=\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\Phi_{t}(x^{i})\big)\right]\bigg|_{s=0}
=Φt​(X~​(xi))|s=0\displaystyle=\Phi_{t}\big(\widetilde{X}(x^{i})\big)\big|_{s=0}
=φt​(Xt​(xi)).\displaystyle=\varphi_{t}\big(X_{t}(x^{i})\big).

Thus the flow property holds for each xix^{i}.

The flow property for time-independent formal functions generalizes to a flow property for all formal tensor fields (time-independent or dependent):

Proposition 2.11.

If φt\varphi_{t} is any formal isotopy satisfying the flow property (FP) for XtX_{t}, then

dd​t​(φt∗​At)=φt∗​(d​Atd​t+ℒXt​At),∀At∈𝒯t(r,s).\displaystyle\frac{d}{dt}(\varphi_{t}^{*}A_{t})=\varphi_{t}^{*}\left(\frac{dA_{t}}{dt}+\mathcal{L}_{X_{t}}A_{t}\right),\qquad\forall A_{t}\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_{t}.
Proof 2.12.

As pullbacks and Lie derivatives are compatible (Equation (5)), we have

φt∗​(d​Atd​t+ℒXt​At)=φt∗​(ℒ∂∂t+Xt​At)=ℒT​(φt∗​At),where ​T:=φt∗​(∂∂t+Xt).\displaystyle\varphi_{t}^{*}\left(\frac{dA_{t}}{dt}+\mathcal{L}_{X_{t}}A_{t}\right)=\varphi_{t}^{*}\left(\mathcal{L}_{\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}+X_{t}}A_{t}\right)=\mathcal{L}_{T}(\varphi_{t}^{*}A_{t}),\quad\text{where }T:=\varphi_{t}^{*}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}+X_{t}\right).

Thus it suffices to show that ∂∂t=T\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}=T, or equivalently, that ∂∂t∘φt=φt∘(∂∂t+Xt)\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\circ\varphi_{t}=\varphi_{t}\circ\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}+X_{t}\right) as operators on 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]. Given f∈𝕂​[[t,x]]f\in\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!], write f​(t,x)=∑k=0∞tk​fk​(x)f(t,x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}f_{k}(x) where each fk​(x)∈𝕂​[[x]]f_{k}(x)\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] does not depend on tt. As ∂∂t\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}} and φt\varphi_{t} are continuous and φt\varphi_{t} fixes tt, we have

∂∂t​(φt​(f))\displaystyle\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\varphi_{t}(f)\big) =∑k∂∂t​(tk​φt​(fk))=∑kk​tk−1​φt​(fk)+∑ktk​∂∂t​(φt​(fk)).\displaystyle=\sum_{k}\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(t^{k}\varphi_{t}(f_{k})\big)=\sum_{k}kt^{k-1}\varphi_{t}(f_{k})+\sum_{k}t^{k}\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\varphi_{t}(f_{k})\big).

The first sum is equal to φt​(∂f∂t)\varphi_{t}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial t}\right). For the second sum, applying the flow property to each fkf_{k} yields

tk​∂∂t​(φt​(fk))=tk​φt​(Xt​(fk))=φt​(tk​Xt​(fk))=φt​(Xt​(tk​fk)),\displaystyle t^{k}\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\varphi_{t}(f_{k})\big)=t^{k}\varphi_{t}\big(X_{t}(f_{k})\big)=\varphi_{t}\big(t^{k}X_{t}(f_{k})\big)=\varphi_{t}\big(X_{t}(t^{k}f_{k})\big),

the last step using the fact that Xt​(tk)=k​tk−1​Xt​(t)=0X_{t}(t^{k})=kt^{k-1}X_{t}(t)=0 and hence Xt​(tk​fk)=tk​Xt​(fk)X_{t}(t^{k}f_{k})=t^{k}X_{t}(f_{k}). Substituting this into the previous calculation yields

∂∂t​(φt​(f))=φt​(∂f∂t)+φt​(Xt​(f))=φt​(∂f∂t+Xt​(f)).\displaystyle\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\varphi_{t}(f)\big)=\varphi_{t}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial t}\right)+\varphi_{t}\big(X_{t}(f)\big)=\varphi_{t}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial t}+X_{t}(f)\right).
Corollary 2.13.

If φt\varphi_{t} is the flow of XtX_{t}, then φt−1\varphi_{t}^{-1} is the flow of −φt∗​Xt-\varphi_{t}^{*}X_{t}.

Proof 2.14.

By Proposition 2.5 and the initial condition (IC), we have (φt−1)0=(φ0)−1=id𝕂​[[x]](\varphi_{t}^{-1})_{0}=(\varphi_{0})^{-1}=\operatorname{id}_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]}. The proof of Proposition 2.11 showed that φt∗​(∂∂t+Xt)=∂∂t\varphi_{t}^{*}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}+X_{t}\right)=\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}; post-composing with φt−1\varphi_{t}^{-1} yields

(∂∂t+Xt)∘φt−1=φt−1∘∂∂t.\displaystyle\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}+X_{t}\right)\circ\varphi_{t}^{-1}=\varphi_{t}^{-1}\circ\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}.

For any time-independent formal function f∈𝕂​[[x]]f\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!], the right-hand side vanishes, hence

∂∂t​(φt−1​(f))=−Xt​(φt−1​(f))=φt−1​((−φt∗​Xt)​(f)).\displaystyle\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\varphi_{t}^{-1}(f)\big)=-X_{t}\big(\varphi_{t}^{-1}(f)\big)=\varphi_{t}^{-1}\big((-\varphi_{t}^{*}X_{t})(f)\big).

Thus φt−1\varphi_{t}^{-1} satisfies the flow property (FP) for −φt∗​Xt-\varphi_{t}^{*}X_{t}, hence is the flow of −φt∗​Xt-\varphi_{t}^{*}X_{t} by uniqueness.

Remark 2.15.

We recount an argument that explains where the exponential formula comes from, as similar strategies will be employed to prove later results. If φt\varphi_{t} is any formal isotopy satisfying the initial condition (IC) and flow property (FP) for a time-independent formal vector field XX, then by the (r,s)=(1,0)(r,s)=(1,0) case of Proposition 2.11,

dd​t​(φt∗​X)=φt∗​(d​Xd​t+[X,X])=0.\displaystyle\frac{d}{dt}(\varphi_{t}^{*}X)=\varphi_{t}^{*}\left(\frac{dX}{dt}+[X,X]\right)=0.

Thus φt∗​X=(φt∗​X)|t=0=X\varphi_{t}^{*}X=(\varphi_{t}^{*}X)|_{t=0}=X by Equation (6) and the initial condition. As φt\varphi_{t} is continuous and fixes tt, it suffices to determine how it acts on the subalgebra 𝕂​[[x]]⊂𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\subset\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]. Given f∈𝕂​[[x]]f\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!], write φt​(f)=∑k=0∞tk​fk\varphi_{t}(f)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}f_{k} where each fk∈𝕂​[[x]]f_{k}\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] does not depend on tt. Then the left-hand side of (FP) is

∂∂t​(φt​(f))=∑k=0∞∂∂t​(tk​fk)=∑k=0∞(k+1)​tk​fk+1,\displaystyle\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\varphi_{t}(f)\big)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(t^{k}f_{k}\big)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(k+1)t^{k}f_{k+1},

while the right-hand side is

φt​(X​(f))\displaystyle\varphi_{t}\big(X(f)\big) =(φt∗​X)​(φt∗​f)=X​(φt​(f))=∑k=0∞tk​X​(fk).\displaystyle=(\varphi_{t}^{*}X)(\varphi_{t}^{*}f)=X\big(\varphi_{t}(f)\big)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}X(f_{k}).

For the flow property to hold, the coefficient power series of tkt^{k} on each side must be equal for all kk, from which we deduce the recursion relation (k+1)​fk+1=X​(fk)(k+1)f_{k+1}=X(f_{k}). The base case f0=φ0​(f)=ff_{0}=\varphi_{0}(f)=f is provided by (IC); it follows by induction that fk=1k!​X∘k​(f)f_{k}=\frac{1}{k!}X^{\circ k}(f), hence φt​(f)=et​X​(f)\varphi_{t}(f)=e^{tX}(f).

2.4 Fixed-time evaluation

We now revisit the fundamental problem posed in the introduction—or rather, its formal analogue:

Given formal tensor fields A0,A1∈𝒯(r,s)A_{0},A_{1}\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}, when does there exist a formal diffeomorphism φ:𝕂​[[x]]→𝕂​[[x]]\varphi\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] such that φ∗​A1=A0\varphi^{*}A_{1}=A_{0}?

To emulate Moser’s trick, one would like to view A0A_{0} and A1A_{1} as time slices at t=0t=0 and t=1t=1 of a one-parameter family of formal tensor fields At:𝕂→𝒯(r,s)A_{t}\colon\mathbb{K}\to\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}; ditto for φ\varphi. However, Moser-type arguments involve equations such as

dd​t​(φt∗​At)=0,\displaystyle\frac{d}{dt}(\varphi_{t}^{*}A_{t})=0,

and it is unclear how to make sense of time derivatives of arbitrary functions 𝕂→𝒯(r,s)\mathbb{K}\to\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)} over an arbitrary field of characteristic zero. On the other hand, the work of Section 2.2 gives meaning to this equation if At∈𝒯t(r,s)A_{t}\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_{t} is a time-dependent formal tensor field and φt:𝕂​[[t,x]]→𝕂​[[t,x]]\varphi_{t}\colon\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] is a formal isotopy, and Section 2.3 allows us to say that this equation is equivalent to

d​Atd​t+ℒXt​At=0,where Xt∈𝔛t generates φt (assuming that φ0=id𝕂​[[x]]).\displaystyle\frac{dA_{t}}{dt}+\mathcal{L}_{X_{t}}A_{t}=0,\qquad\text{where $X_{t}\in\mathfrak{X}_{t}$ generates $\varphi_{t}$ (assuming that $\varphi_{0}=\operatorname{id}_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]}$).}

But then comes another issue, which we have already alluded to: while time slices of AtA_{t}, φt\varphi_{t}, and XtX_{t} at t=0t=0 are perfectly well-defined, replacing the formal variable tt with a non-zero τ∈𝕂\tau\in\mathbb{K} immediately leads to a veritable laundry list of complications. We further illustrate how things can go wrong with examples in dimension n=1n=1 (and the formal variable x1x^{1} relabelled as xx).

Example 2.16.

Even if substituting t=τt=\tau into a formal isotopy yields well-defined power series in xx, the result need not define a formal diffeomorphism.
