For instance, the flow φt\varphi_{t} of the formal vector field ∂∂x\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}} is given by φt​(x)=x+t\varphi_{t}(x)=x+t. Replacing tt with any non-zero τ∈𝕂\tau\in\mathbb{K} yields a power series in xx with non-zero constant term, which cannot correspond to a formal diffeomorphism of 𝕂\mathbb{K}.

Example 2.17.

Even if substituting t=τt=\tau into a formal isotopy and time-dependent formal tensor field both make sense, the pullback need not satisfy the same property. For instance, consider the formal isotopy φt\varphi_{t} of 𝕂\mathbb{K} given by φt​(x)=x​(1−t)\varphi_{t}(x)=x(1-t). Since φt−1​(x)=x​(1−t)−1=x​∑k=0∞tk\varphi_{t}^{-1}(x)=x(1-t)^{-1}=x\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}, the pullback of the formal vector field ∂∂x\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}} is

φt∗​(∂∂x)=∑k=0∞tk​∂∂x,\displaystyle\varphi_{t}^{*}\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}}\right)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}},

which does not make sense at t=1t=1. (Relatedly, substituting t=1t=1 into x​(1−t)x(1-t) yields the zero power series, which does not correspond to a formal diffeomorphism of 𝕂\mathbb{K}.)

Example 2.18.

Regarding the Euler vector field ℰ=x​∂∂x\mathcal{E}=x\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}} as a formal vector field, its flow φt\varphi_{t} is given by

φt​(x)=∑k=0∞tkk!​x.\displaystyle\varphi_{t}(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{t^{k}}{k!}x.

One might try to make sense of “substituting t=1t=1 into φt\varphi_{t}” by declaring φ1\varphi_{1} to be the formal diffeomorphism of 𝕂\mathbb{K} corresponding to the reasonable-looking expression ∑k=0∞1k!​x\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{k!}x. However, this infinite sum does not converge in the 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adic topology on 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!], as Θ​(1k!​x)=1\Theta(\frac{1}{k!}x)=1 for all kk (c.f. the beginning of Section 2.1). If the base field 𝕂\mathbb{K} is topologized so that ∑k1k!\sum_{k}\frac{1}{k!} converges to a unique e∈𝕂e\in\mathbb{K} (e.g., 𝕂\mathbb{K} contains the real number e=2.718​…e=2.718\ldots), then one could declare φ1\varphi_{1} to be the formal diffeomorphism given by the power series φ1​(x):=e​x∈𝕂​[[x]]\varphi_{1}(x):=ex\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]. We will not do this for a few reasons.

For one, 𝕂\mathbb{K} need not come with a topology; even if it does, there may be power series that diverge for all non-zero τ∈𝕂\tau\in\mathbb{K}.333Euler gives an example of how the series ∑k=1∞(k−1)!​xk\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}(k-1)!x^{k}, which diverges for all non-zero x∈ℝx\in\mathbb{R}, appears when studying the system of differential equations x˙=x2\dot{x}=x^{2} and y˙=y−x\dot{y}=y-x [2, §24]. More philosophically, using a topology on 𝕂\mathbb{K} is incongruous with our approach. Determining whether a given infinite sum converges is generally a difficult task, and the entire raison d’être for working with formal power series is to blithely ignore such concerns. Indeed, under the identification of sets 𝕂​[[x]]≅𝕂ℕn\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\cong\mathbb{K}^{\mathbb{N}^{n}} associating ∑αcα​xα∈𝕂​[[x]]\sum_{\alpha}c_{\alpha}x^{\alpha}\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] to the function ℕn→𝕂\mathbb{N}^{n}\to\mathbb{K} given by α↦cα\alpha\mapsto c_{\alpha}, the 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adic topology on 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] corresponds to the product topology on 𝕂ℕn\mathbb{K}^{\mathbb{N}^{n}} when 𝕂\mathbb{K} is equipped with the discrete topology. Thus 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] does not see any interesting topology on 𝕂\mathbb{K} anyways.

Fundamentally, the problem is that we are trying to interpret formal power series f∈𝕂​[[t,x]]f\in\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] as “functions” 𝕂→𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!], t↦f​(t,x)t\mapsto f(t,x) and “evaluate” them at non-zero τ∈𝕂\tau\in\mathbb{K} without using a topology on 𝕂\mathbb{K}. A simple way to circumvent the issues that arise is by requiring these “functions” to be polynomial in tt, i.e., within the subalgebra 𝕂​[[x]]​[t]⊂𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!][t]\subset\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]. While such polynomials can be regarded as honest-to-goodness functions 𝕂→𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!], this turns out to be too restrictive—for instance, non-zero formal vector fields never integrate to 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra automorphisms of 𝕂​[[x]]​[t]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!][t] (see Remark 2.31). An intermediate approach is to restrict attention to those formal power series such that, for each fixed α∈ℕn\alpha\in\mathbb{N}^{n}, the coefficient of the monomial xαx^{\alpha} depends polynomially on tt (but the degrees of the polynomials may be unbounded as α\alpha varies). This is essentially what we shall do, with some additional conditions for formal isotopies:

Definition 2.19.

We define evaluative formal tensor fields and formal isotopies as follows. Firstly, a time-dependent formal function f​(t,x)∈𝕂​[[t,x]]f(t,x)\in\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] is called evaluative if f​(t,x)∈𝕂​[t]​[[x]]f(t,x)\in\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!]:

f​(t,x)=∑α∈ℕnpα​(t)​xα, where each pα​(t) is a polynomial in t (as opposed to a power series).\displaystyle f(t,x)=\sum_{\alpha\in\mathbb{N}^{n}}p_{\alpha}(t)x^{\alpha},\text{ where each $p_{\alpha}(t)$ is a polynomial in $t$ (as opposed to a power series).}

Next, a time-dependent formal tensor field At∈𝒯t(r,s)A_{t}\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_{t} is called evaluative if all of its component power series are evaluative. Lastly, a formal isotopy φt\varphi_{t}, identified with an nn-tuple φ​(t,x)=(φ1​(t,x),…,φn​(t,x))∈𝕂​[[t,x]]n\varphi(t,x)=\big(\varphi^{1}(t,x),\ldots,\varphi^{n}(t,x)\big)\in\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]^{n}, is called evaluative if both of the following conditions hold:

  1. (i)

    All nn component power series φi​(t,x)\varphi^{i}(t,x) are evaluative and satisfy φi​(t,0)=0\varphi^{i}(t,0)=0.

  2. (ii)

    The matrix Dx​φ​(t,x)D_{x}\varphi(t,x) is invertible in the ring of n×nn\times n matrices with entries in 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!]. Equivalently, Dx​φ​(t,0)D_{x}\varphi(t,0) is invertible in the ring of n×nn\times n matrices with entries in 𝕂​[t]\mathbb{K}[t], or detDx​φ​(t,0)\det D_{x}\varphi(t,0) is a non-zero constant polynomial.

Example 2.20.

Let p∈𝕂​[t]p\in\mathbb{K}[t] be any polynomial and define P​(t):=∫0tp​(s)​𝑑s∈𝕂​[t]P(t):=\int_{0}^{t}p(s)\,ds\in\mathbb{K}[t]. In dimension n=1n=1 with a single formal variable xx, the time-dependent formal vector field p​(t)​x2​∂∂xp(t)x^{2}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}} is evaluative. Its flow corresponds to the evaluative power series (which is not in 𝕂​[[x]]​[t]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!][t] unless p=0p=0)

φ​(t,x):=x​(1−P​(t)​x)−1=∑k=0∞P​(t)k​xk+1.\displaystyle\varphi(t,x):=x(1-P(t)x)^{-1}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}P(t)^{k}x^{k+1}.

Since φ​(t,0)=0\varphi(t,0)=0 and Dx​φ​(t,0)=1D_{x}\varphi(t,0)=1, the flow of p​(t)​x2​∂∂xp(t)x^{2}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}} is an evaluative formal isotopy. In general, if the flow φt\varphi_{t} of a time-dependent formal vector field XtX_{t} is evaluative, then XtX_{t} is evaluative: solve ∂∂t​(φt​(xi))=φt​(Xt​(xi))\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\varphi_{t}(x^{i})\big)=\varphi_{t}\big(X_{t}(x^{i})\big) for Xt​(xi)=φt−1​(∂∂t​φt​(xi))X_{t}(x^{i})=\varphi_{t}^{-1}\big(\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\varphi_{t}(x^{i})\big) and use Proposition 2.22 below. However, an evaluative (or even time-independent) formal vector field need not have evaluative flow, as demonstrated by Examples 2.16 and 2.18.

For any τ∈𝕂\tau\in\mathbb{K}, the map 𝕂​[t]→𝕂\mathbb{K}[t]\to\mathbb{K} given by evaluation of polynomials at t=τt=\tau extends coefficient-wise to a 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]-algebra homomorphism

evalτ:𝕂​[t]​[[x]]→𝕂​[[x]],f​(t,x)↦f​(τ,x).\displaystyle\operatorname{eval}_{\tau}\colon\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!],\qquad f(t,x)\mapsto f(\tau,x).

Given an evaluative formal tensor field AtA_{t}, we denote the time-independent formal tensor field obtained by evaluating each of its components at t=τt=\tau by AτA_{\tau} (or At|t=τA_{t}|_{t=\tau}, if confusion is possible). Given an evaluative formal isotopy φt\varphi_{t} with corresponding nn-tuple φ​(t,x)=(φ1​(t,x),…,φn​(t,x))∈𝕂​[[t,x]]n\varphi(t,x)=\big(\varphi^{1}(t,x),\ldots,\varphi^{n}(t,x)\big)\in\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]^{n}, condition (i) guarantees that φ​(τ,x):=(φ1​(τ,x),…,φn​(τ,x))∈𝕂​[[x]]n\varphi(\tau,x):=\big(\varphi^{1}(\tau,x),\ldots,\varphi^{n}(\tau,x)\big)\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]^{n} is well-defined and corresponds to a unique 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra endomorphism of 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] that we denote by φτ\varphi_{\tau} or φt|t=τ\varphi_{t}|_{t=\tau}; condition (ii) implies that φτ\varphi_{\tau} is a formal diffeomorphism. In particular, At|t=0A_{t}|_{t=0} and φt|t=0\varphi_{t}|_{t=0} respectively agree with the time zero slices A0A_{0} and φ0\varphi_{0} as defined in Section 2.2.

Remark 2.21.

One might consider weakening condition (ii) by only requiring detDx​φ​(τ,0)\det D_{x}\varphi(\tau,0) to be a non-zero element of 𝕂\mathbb{K} for each fixed τ∈𝕂\tau\in\mathbb{K}; combined with condition (i), this still implies that φτ\varphi_{\tau} is a formal diffeomorphism. However, even if the polynomial function τ↦detDx​φ​(τ,0)\tau\mapsto\det D_{x}\varphi(\tau,0) is nowhere-vanishing on 𝕂\mathbb{K}, it can (and must, if non-constant) have zeroes in a field extension of 𝕂\mathbb{K}, and this can lead to undesirable phenomena. For example, the formal isotopy in dimension n=1n=1 given by φt​(x)=x​(t2+1)\varphi_{t}(x)=x(t^{2}+1) satisfies condition (i) and this weaker version of condition (ii) over ℝ\mathbb{R}, but not over ℂ\mathbb{C}; its inverse does not satisfy condition (i) as φt−1​(x)=x​(t2+1)−1=x​∑k=0∞(−1)k​t2​k\varphi_{t}^{-1}(x)=x(t^{2}+1)^{-1}=x\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(-1)^{k}t^{2k} is not evaluative.

For completeness, we also note that there is no benefit to relaxing condition (i) by only requiring that φi​(τ,0)=0\varphi^{i}(\tau,0)=0 for each fixed τ∈𝕂\tau\in\mathbb{K}: since 𝕂\mathbb{K} is an infinite field, this is equivalent to φi​(t,0)\varphi^{i}(t,0) being the zero polynomial.

A formal isotopy is evaluative if and only if it is the extension of a 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra automorphism of 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!] that fixes tt, i.e., a 𝕂​[t]\mathbb{K}[t]-algebra automorphism of 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!]. A time-dependent formal vector field is evaluative if and only if it is the extension of a derivation of 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!] that vanishes on tt, i.e., a 𝕂​[t]\mathbb{K}[t]-linear derivation of 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!]. (See Remark 2.23 for more details.) In theory, we could repeat the developments of Sections 2.1 and 2.2 with 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!] in mind, mutatis mutandis. We list, without proof, some results about evaluative objects obtained by doing so:

Proposition 2.22.

Fix At∈𝒯t(r,s)A_{t}\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_{t}, a formal isotopy φt\varphi_{t}, and τ∈𝕂\tau\in\mathbb{K}.

  1. (i)

    If φt\varphi_{t} is evaluative, then so is φt−1\varphi_{t}^{-1}. If ψt\psi_{t} is also an evaluative formal isotopy, then so is ψt∘φt\psi_{t}\circ\varphi_{t}. Evaluation at t=τt=\tau commutes with inversion and composition:

    (φt−1)|t=τ=(φτ)−1and(ψt∘φt)|t=τ=ψτ∘φτ.\displaystyle(\varphi_{t}^{-1})|_{t=\tau}=(\varphi_{\tau})^{-1}\quad\text{and}\quad(\psi_{t}\circ\varphi_{t})|_{t=\tau}=\psi_{\tau}\circ\varphi_{\tau}.
  2. (ii)

    If φt\varphi_{t} and AtA_{t} are both evaluative, then so is φt∗​At\varphi_{t}^{*}A_{t}. Evaluation commutes with pullback:

    (φt∗​At)|t=τ=(φτ)∗​(Aτ).\displaystyle(\varphi_{t}^{*}A_{t})|_{t=\tau}=(\varphi_{\tau})^{*}(A_{\tau}).
  3. (iii)

    Tensor products, contractions, and time derivatives of evaluative formal tensor fields are evaluative. Evaluation commutes with tensor products and contractions (but not with dd​t!\frac{d}{dt}!).

Remark 2.23.

Let II be the ideal of 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!] generated by the xix^{i}’s, and let JJ be the ideal of 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!] generated by tt and the xix^{i}’s.444Maximal ideals of 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!] are precisely those generated by an irreducible polynomial p​(t)∈𝕂​[t]p(t)\in\mathbb{K}[t] and the xix^{i}’s, so JJ is maximal while II is not. The II-adic topology on 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!] is the typical choice for topologizing a power series algebra, while the JJ-adic topology coincides with the subspace topology inherited from the 𝔪t,x\mathfrak{m}_{t,x}-adic topology on 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]. The II-adic topology is strictly finer than the JJ-adic topology. For instance, the sequence (tk)(t^{k}) does not converge in the II-adic topology, but it converges to 0 in the JJ-adic topology. As a consequence, evalτ:𝕂​[t]​[[x]]→𝕂​[[x]]\operatorname{eval}_{\tau}\colon\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] is continuous when 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!] is equipped with the II-adic topology, but is discontinuous for τ≠0\tau\neq 0 when 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!] is equipped with the JJ-adic topology (as the sequence (τk)(\tau^{k}) does not converge in the 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adic topology of 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]).

The difference turns out to not matter much for the purposes of evaluative objects. Indeed, 𝕂​[t]\mathbb{K}[t]-linear derivations of 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!] are automatically continuous with respect to the II-adic topology, from which it follows that they form a free 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!]-module with basis {∂∂xi}\left\{\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}\right\}—the proof is exactly the same as for derivations of 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]. Writing out any X∈Der𝕂​[t]⁡(𝕂​[t]​[[x]])X\in\operatorname{Der}_{\mathbb{K}[t]}(\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!]) in this basis and extending the coordinate expression to act on power series in tt, one obtains a derivation of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]. This extension is continuous in the 𝔪t,x\mathfrak{m}_{t,x}-adic topology, so XX is also continuous with respect to the subspace (i.e., JJ-adic) topology.

Similarly, a 𝕂​[t]\mathbb{K}[t]-algebra endomorphism φ\varphi of 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!] is automatically continuous with respect to both the II-adic and JJ-adic topologies, but we cannot appeal to Remark 2.1 to justify this as 𝕂​[t]\mathbb{K}[t] is not a field. Instead, we use a degree argument. For each i∈{1,…,n}i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}, 1−t​xi1-tx^{i} is invertible in 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!] as its constant term (with respect to the xix^{i}’s) is invertible in 𝕂​[t]\mathbb{K}[t]; thus φ​(1−t​xi)=1−t​φ​(xi)\varphi(1-tx^{i})=1-t\varphi(x^{i}) is also invertible in 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!]. If pi​(t)∈𝕂​[t]p^{i}(t)\in\mathbb{K}[t] denotes the constant term of φ​(xi)\varphi(x^{i}), then the constant term of 1−t​φ​(xi)1-t\varphi(x^{i}) is 1−t​pi​(t)1-tp^{i}(t), which is therefore invertible in 𝕂​[t]\mathbb{K}[t]. By degree considerations, we see that pi​(t)=0p^{i}(t)=0, hence φ​(xi)∈I\varphi(x^{i})\in I. As φ​(t)=t\varphi(t)=t, it follows that φ​(I)⊆I\varphi(I)\subseteq I and φ​(J)⊆J\varphi(J)\subseteq J, so φ\varphi is both II-adically and JJ-adically continuous. For details on what can happen if one relaxes 𝕂​[t]\mathbb{K}[t]-linearity to just 𝕂\mathbb{K}-linearity, see [13].

If At∈𝒯t(r,s)A_{t}\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_{t} is time-independent (d​Atd​t=0\frac{dA_{t}}{dt}=0), then it is in particular evaluative, so we may identify AtA_{t} with any of its fixed time slices Aτ∈𝒯(r,s)A_{\tau}\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)} where τ∈𝕂\tau\in\mathbb{K}. (In Section 2.2, we did this for τ=0\tau=0 only.) We have thus nailed down definitions to make sense of a formal version of Moser’s trick, which will differ in appearance from the version in Section 1.2 for reasons to be explained shortly:

Corollary 2.24 (Formal Moser trick).

If At∈𝒯t(r,s)A_{t}\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)}_{t} is an evaluative formal tensor field and φt:𝕂​[[t,x]]→𝕂​[[t,x]]\varphi_{t}\colon\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] is an evaluative formal isotopy such that dd​t​(φt∗​At)=0\frac{d}{dt}(\varphi_{t}^{*}A_{t})=0 and φ0=id𝕂​[[x]]\varphi_{0}=\operatorname{id}_{\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]}, then the time-independent formal tensor fields A0,A1∈𝒯(r,s)A_{0},A_{1}\in\mathcal{T}^{(r,s)} and the formal diffeomorphism φ1:𝕂​[[x]]→𝕂​[[x]]\varphi_{1}\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] satisfy φ1∗​A1=A0\varphi_{1}^{*}A_{1}=A_{0}.

Proof 2.25.

Since dd​t​(φt∗​At)=0\frac{d}{dt}(\varphi_{t}^{*}A_{t})=0, we have (φ1)∗​A1=(φt∗​At)|t=1=(φt∗​At)|t=0=A0(\varphi_{1})^{*}A_{1}=(\varphi_{t}^{*}A_{t})|_{t=1}=(\varphi_{t}^{*}A_{t})|_{t=0}=A_{0}.

We note that differential forms and multivector fields can also be imported into the formal setting by considering exterior powers of the 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]-modules Ω1\Omega^{1} and 𝔛\mathfrak{X}. Naturally, one can then proceed to develop the full apparatus of a formal Cartan calculus (exterior differentials, interior products, Lie derivatives, Schouten brackets, etc.), as well as time-dependent and evaluative versions, thus obtaining a formal Moser trick for these as well.

We stated the formal Moser trick in terms of a formal isotopy φt\varphi_{t} satisfying dd​t​(φt∗​At)=0\frac{d}{dt}(\varphi_{t}^{*}A_{t})=0, rather than in terms of a time-dependent formal vector field XtX_{t} satisfying d​Atd​t+ℒXt​At=0\frac{dA_{t}}{dt}+\mathcal{L}_{X_{t}}A_{t}=0. This is because, even if XtX_{t} is evaluative, its flow is not guaranteed to be evaluative (Examples 2.16 and 2.18). Combining the next two results yields a sufficient condition for a flow to be evaluative.

Proposition 2.26.

Let φt\varphi_{t} be a formal isotopy of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} with corresponding nn-tuple written in the form φ​(t,x)=∑k=0∞tk​φk​(x)\varphi(t,x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}\varphi_{k}(x) where φk​(x)∈𝕂​[[x]]n\varphi_{k}(x)\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]^{n}. If Θ​(φk)≥2\Theta(\varphi_{k})\geq 2 for all k≥1k\geq 1 and limk→∞Θ​(φk)=∞\lim_{k\to\infty}\Theta(\varphi_{k})=\infty, then φt\varphi_{t} is evaluative.

Remark 2.27 (a partial converse).

If an nn-tuple φ​(t,x)=∑ktk​φk​(x)\varphi(t,x)=\sum_{k}t^{k}\varphi_{k}(x) corresponds to a formal isotopy that is evaluative, then Θ​(φk)≥1\Theta(\varphi_{k})\geq 1 for all k≥1k\geq 1 and limk→∞Θ​(φk)=∞\lim_{k\to\infty}\Theta(\varphi_{k})=\infty, but it is not necessarily true that Θ​(φk)≥2\Theta(\varphi_{k})\geq 2 for k≥1k\geq 1, as demonstrated by the evaluative formal isotopy of 𝕂2\mathbb{K}^{2} corresponding to the 22-tuple (x+t​y,y)(x+ty,y) (the flow of y​∂∂xy\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}}).

Proposition 2.28.

Suppose Xt∈𝔛tX_{t}\in\mathfrak{X}_{t} is written in the form Xt=∑k=0∞tk​XkX_{t}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}X_{k} where Xk∈𝔛X_{k}\in\mathfrak{X}. Identify the flow φt\varphi_{t} of XtX_{t} with an nn-tuple φ​(t,x)=∑k=0∞tk​φk​(x)\varphi(t,x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}\varphi_{k}(x) where φk​(x)∈𝕂​[[x]]n\varphi_{k}(x)\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]^{n}. If Θ​(Xk)≥k+2\Theta(X_{k})\geq k+2 for all k≥0k\geq 0 (the Big Theta notation understood by identifying XkX_{k} with (Xk​(x1),…,Xk​(xn))∈𝕂​[[x]]n\left(X_{k}(x^{1}),\ldots,X_{k}(x^{n})\right)\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]^{n}), then Θ​(φk)≥k+1\Theta(\varphi_{k})\geq k+1 for all k≥0k\geq 0. In particular, φt\varphi_{t} is an evaluative formal isotopy by Proposition 2.26.

Notice that the Euler vector field ℰ=x​∂∂x\mathcal{E}=x\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}} from Example 2.18 just barely fails the hypothesis for k=0k=0, but the conclusion drastically fails: the power series ∑ktkk!​x\sum_{k}\frac{t^{k}}{k!}x has Θ​(φk)=1\Theta(\varphi_{k})=1 for all kk.

We will prove weighted generalizations of both Propositions 2.26 and 2.28 in Section 3 (Propositions 3.4 and 3.6). As such, we will not prove the former at all, and we will only prove the latter in dimension n=1n=1 to convey the main ideas of the more general proof; as we shall see, the argument for n>1n>1 is analogous but requires significantly more complicated notation.

Proof 2.29 (Proof in dimension n=1n=1).

We proceed by (strong) induction on kk. By the initial condition (IC), we have φ0​(x)=x\varphi_{0}(x)=x, hence Θ​(φ0)=1\Theta(\varphi_{0})=1. This establishes the base case; for the inductive step, assume that Θ​(φk)≥k+1\Theta(\varphi_{k})\geq k+1 for all k≤mk\leq m. The flow property (FP) with f​(x)=xf(x)=x reads

∂∂t​(φt​(x))=φt​(Xt​(x)),\displaystyle\frac{\partial}{\partial{t}}\big(\varphi_{t}(x)\big)=\varphi_{t}\big(X_{t}(x)\big),

and as observed in Remark 2.15, the coefficient of tmt^{m} on the left-hand side is (m+1)​φm+1​(x)(m+1)\varphi_{m+1}(x). Thus it suffices to determine the corresponding coefficient of tmt^{m} on the right-hand side and show that its order is at least m+2m+2. As Θ​(Xk)≥k+2\Theta(X_{k})\geq k+2 for all kk, we can write Xk=∑α=k+2∞ck,α​xα​∂∂xX_{k}=\sum_{\alpha=k+2}^{\infty}c_{k,\alpha}x^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}} for some coefficients ck,α∈𝕂c_{k,\alpha}\in\mathbb{K}. As φt\varphi_{t} is a 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra automorphism fixing tt, we thus have

φt​(Xt​(x))=∑k=0∞∑α=k+2∞ck,α​tk​φt​(x)α.\displaystyle\varphi_{t}\big(X_{t}(x)\big)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\sum_{\alpha=k+2}^{\infty}c_{k,\alpha}t^{k}\varphi_{t}(x)^{\alpha}.

The term φt​(x)α\varphi_{t}(x)^{\alpha} can be expressed as a sum over multi-indices of length α\alpha:

φt​(x)α=(∑ℓ=0∞tℓ​φℓ​(x))α=∑β∈ℕαt|β|​∏ℓ=1αφβℓ​(x),\displaystyle\varphi_{t}(x)^{\alpha}=\Bigg(\sum_{\ell=0}^{\infty}t^{\ell}\varphi_{\ell}(x)\Bigg)^{\alpha}=\sum_{\beta\in\mathbb{N}^{\alpha}}t^{|\beta|}\prod_{\ell=1}^{\alpha}\varphi_{\beta_{\ell}}(x),

from which it follows that the coefficient of tmt^{m} in φt​(Xt​(x))\varphi_{t}(X_{t}(x)) is

∑k=0∞∑α=k+2∞∑β∈ℕα|β|+k=m(ck,α​∏ℓ=1αφβℓ​(x)).\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\sum_{\alpha=k+2}^{\infty}\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}\beta\in\mathbb{N}^{\alpha}\\ |\beta|+k=m\end{subarray}}\left(c_{k,\alpha}\prod_{\ell=1}^{\alpha}\varphi_{\beta_{\ell}}(x)\right).

Focusing on the bracketed expression for each fixed kk, α\alpha, and β\beta, observe that βℓ≤|β|≤m\beta_{\ell}\leq|\beta|\leq m, hence Θ​(φβℓ)≥βℓ+1\Theta(\varphi_{\beta_{\ell}})\geq\beta_{\ell}+1 by the induction hypothesis. As 𝕂\mathbb{K} is an integral domain, the order of a product of power series is the sum of the orders; thus

Θ​(∏ℓ=1αφβℓ)=∑ℓ=1αΘ​(φβℓ)≥|β|+α≥|β|+k+2=m+2.\displaystyle\Theta\left(\prod_{\ell=1}^{\alpha}\varphi_{\beta_{\ell}}\right)=\sum_{\ell=1}^{\alpha}\Theta(\varphi_{\beta_{\ell}})\geq|\beta|+\alpha\geq|\beta|+k+2=m+2.

As the coefficient of tmt^{m} in φt​(Xt​(x))\varphi_{t}\big(X_{t}(x)\big) is a sum of terms which all have order ≥m+2\geq m+2, it follows that Θ​(φm+1)≥m+2\Theta(\varphi_{m+1})\geq m+2, completing the induction.

We conclude this section by drawing some comparisons between evaluative vector fields, which are certain derivations of 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!], and two well-known types of derivations of other algebras resulting from taking a mix of polynomials and power series in several variables; in particular, what happens when one swaps the roles of tt and xx from 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!] to 𝕂​[[t]]​[x]\mathbb{K}[\![t]\!][x] and 𝕂​[[x]]​[t]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!][t].

Remark 2.30.

A poly-flow vector field is a time-independent C1C^{1} vector field XX, defined on some open set U⊆ℝnU\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{n} but not assumed to be complete, whose flow φt\varphi_{t} is “locally polynomial in xx” in the following sense:

There exists an open set Ω⊆ℝn+1\Omega\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{n+1} such that Ω\Omega is contained inside the maximal flow domain of XX, Ω\Omega contains {0}×U\{0\}\times U, and for each t∈ℝt\in\mathbb{R}, the time tt flow φt\varphi_{t} restricts to a polynomial mapping Ut,Ω→ℝnU_{t,\Omega}\to\mathbb{R}^{n} whenever Ut,Ω:={x∈U∣(t,x)∈Ω}U_{t,\Omega}:=\{x\in U\mid(t,x)\in\Omega\} is non-empty.

Bass–Meisters [3, Theorem 4.1] have shown that φt\varphi_{t} being locally polynomial in xx automatically implies, among other facts, that both XX and φt\varphi_{t} are globally polynomial in xx. (Compare with how the flow of a time-dependent formal vector field being evaluative implies that the vector field itself is evaluative, as noted in Example 2.20.)

More precisely, they prove that the component functions of XX are polynomial functions restricted to UU; extending these polynomial functions yields a vector field X~\widetilde{X} on all of ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n} that is complete; for all t∈ℝt\in\mathbb{R}, the time tt flow φ~t:ℝn→ℝn\widetilde{\varphi}_{t}\colon\mathbb{R}^{n}\to\mathbb{R}^{n} of X~\widetilde{X} is polynomial in xx. Moreover, the degrees of these polynomials are bounded by some fixed integer as tt varies, and the coefficients of these polynomials are analytic functions of tt (Coomes [8, Theorem 1.1] later proved that the extended flow φ~:ℝ×ℝn→ℝn\widetilde{\varphi}\colon\mathbb{R}\times\mathbb{R}^{n}\to\mathbb{R}^{n} extends further to a holomorphic function ℂ×ℂn→ℂn\mathbb{C}\times\mathbb{C}^{n}\to\mathbb{C}^{n} satisfying the flow group property with respect to the complex time parameter). In particular, X~\widetilde{X} can be regarded as a derivation of the polynomial algebra ℝ​[x1,…,xn]\mathbb{R}[x^{1},\ldots,x^{n}], and the formal Taylor series of the components of φ~t\widetilde{\varphi}_{t} are in ℝ​[[t]]​[x1,…,xn]\mathbb{R}[\![t]\!][x^{1},\ldots,x^{n}]. Bass–Meisters also prove that detDx​φ~​(t,x)\det D_{x}\widetilde{\varphi}(t,x) depends only on tt and not xx, hence is equal to detDx​φ~​(t,0)\det D_{x}\widetilde{\varphi}(t,0). This is somewhat opposite to our definition of evaluative isotopies, in which we allow the Jacobian determinant to depend on xx, but not on tt when x=0x=0.

If 𝕂\mathbb{K} is any characteristic zero field and f:𝕂n→𝕂nf\colon\mathbb{K}^{n}\to\mathbb{K}^{n} is a bijective function such that the component functions of both ff and f−1f^{-1} are all polynomials, then it follows from the multivariable chain rule that detD​f​(x)\det Df(x) is an invertible element of 𝕂​[x1,…,xn]\mathbb{K}[x^{1},\ldots,x^{n}], hence a non-zero constant. The famous Jacobian conjecture is about the converse: if the components of ff are polynomial functions and detD​f​(x)\det Df(x) is a non-zero constant, must ff admit an inverse whose components are also polynomial functions? For 𝕂=ℝ\mathbb{K}=\mathbb{R} and 𝕂=ℂ\mathbb{K}=\mathbb{C}, Meisters–Olech [18] have shown that the Jacobian conjecture holding for a given ff is equivalent to the assertion that certain vector fields defined by ff have flows that are polynomial in both xx and tt (where tt is a complex parameter in the case that 𝕂=ℂ\mathbb{K}=\mathbb{C}).

Remark 2.31.

By replacing 𝕂​[t]​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[t][\![x]\!] with 𝕂​[[x]]​[t]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!][t] in Definition 2.19, one is led to the theory of locally nilpotent derivations [10]: derivations XX of an algebra AA with the property that, for each a∈Aa\in A, there exists an integer kk such that X∘k​(a)=0X^{\circ k}(a)=0. Equivalently, extend XX to a derivation of A​[[t]]A[\![t]\!] by declaring that ∑kX​(ak​tk)=∑kX​(ak)​tk\sum_{k}X(a_{k}t^{k})=\sum_{k}X(a_{k})t^{k}; then XX is locally nilpotent if and only if the algebra automorphism et​X:A​[[t]]→A​[[t]]e^{tX}\colon A[\![t]\!]\to A[\![t]\!] defined exactly as in Theorem 2.8 leaves A​[t]A[t] invariant. However, the only locally nilpotent derivation of A=𝕂​[[x]]A=\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] is the zero derivation.

Indeed, suppose φt\varphi_{t} is any 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra endomorphism of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] that fixes tt and satisfies the initial condition (IC) (e.g., the flow of any X∈𝔛X\in\mathfrak{X}). If 𝕂​[[x]]​[t]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!][t] is invariant under φt\varphi_{t}, then φt​(1−xi)=1−φt​(xi)\varphi_{t}(1-x^{i})=1-\varphi_{t}(x^{i}) is invertible in 𝕂​[[x]]​[t]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!][t] as 1−xi1-x^{i} is invertible in 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]. Thus φt​(xi)\varphi_{t}(x^{i}) must be a constant polynomial with respect to tt; in particular, it is equal to its t=0t=0 time slice, which is xix^{i} by (IC). Since φt\varphi_{t} is a ring homomorphism that fixes tt and each xix^{i}, it restricts to the identity map on the polynomial ring 𝕂​[t,x1,…,xn]\mathbb{K}[t,x^{1},\ldots,x^{n}]. As 𝕂​[t,x1,…,xn]\mathbb{K}[t,x^{1},\ldots,x^{n}] is a dense subset of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] in the (Hausdorff) 𝔪t,x\mathfrak{m}_{t,x}-adic topology and φt\varphi_{t} is continuous by Remark 2.1, it follows that φt\varphi_{t} is the identity map on 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!]. By Remark 2.9, φt\varphi_{t} is the flow of the zero vector field.

3 Weightings of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n}

3.1 The algebra of weights

We continue to use the shorthand notation 𝕂​[[x]]:=𝕂​[[x1,…,xn]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]:=\mathbb{K}[\![x^{1},\ldots,x^{n}]\!] for the algebra of formal power series with coefficients in a field 𝕂\mathbb{K} of characteristic zero, and we also write 𝕂​[x]:=𝕂​[x1,…,xn]\mathbb{K}[x]:=\mathbb{K}[x^{1},\ldots,x^{n}] for the subalgebra of polynomials. A polynomial vector field on 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} is a derivation of 𝕂​[x]\mathbb{K}[x]. The set 𝔛pol\mathfrak{X}_{\mathrm{pol}} of polynomial vector fields is both a module over 𝕂​[x]\mathbb{K}[x] with a basis given by {∂∂x1,…,∂∂xn}\left\{\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{1}}},\ldots,\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{n}}}\right\} and a Lie algebra over 𝕂\mathbb{K}. We may naturally regard 𝔛pol⊂𝔛\mathfrak{X}_{\mathrm{pol}}\subset\mathfrak{X} as a Lie subalgebra of the formal vector fields, and when 𝕂=ℝ\mathbb{K}=\mathbb{R} or 𝕂=ℂ\mathbb{K}=\mathbb{C}, also as a Lie subalgebra of the smooth vector fields on 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n}.

Definition 3.1.

A weighting of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} is an nn-tuple of integers w=(w1,w2,…,wn)w=(w_{1},w_{2},\ldots,w_{n}) such that 1≤w1≤w2≤⋯≤wn1\leq w_{1}\leq w_{2}\leq\dots\leq w_{n}.

We think of the iith weight wiw_{i} as associated with xix^{i} (both the formal variable xi∈𝕂​[x]⊂𝕂​[[x]]x^{i}\in\mathbb{K}[x]\subset\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] and the coordinate function xi:𝕂n→𝕂x^{i}\colon\mathbb{K}^{n}\to\mathbb{K}). By extension, the weighted degree of a monomial xα=(x1)α1​⋯​(xn)αnx^{\alpha}=(x^{1})^{\alpha_{1}}\cdots(x^{n})^{\alpha_{n}} with respect to ww is ⟨w,α⟩:=∑i=1nwi​αi\langle w,\alpha\rangle:=\sum_{i=1}^{n}w_{i}\alpha_{i}. The ideal of 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] generated by monomials xαx^{\alpha} such that ⟨w,α⟩≥k\langle w,\alpha\rangle\geq k is denoted by ℱk\mathcal{F}^{k}.

Requiring weights to be listed from smallest to largest is done out of convenience to simplify proofs (e.g., those of Propositions 3.4 and 4.6). One can of course start with weights in a different order, permute the coordinates of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} to sort them, then apply the results of this section. However, the requirement that weights be strictly positive is not arbitrary: for instance, it guarantees that the translation-invariant topology on 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] determined by the decreasing filtration by ideals

𝕂​[[x]]=ℱ0⊇ℱ1⊇ℱ2⊇⋯\displaystyle\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]=\mathcal{F}^{0}\supseteq\mathcal{F}^{1}\supseteq\mathcal{F}^{2}\supseteq\cdots

coincides with the 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adic topology. (Note that ℱk=𝔪\mathcal{F}^{k}=\mathfrak{m} for 1≤k≤w11\leq k\leq w_{1}, as ℱ1⊆𝔪⊆ℱw1⊆ℱ1\mathcal{F}^{1}\subseteq\mathfrak{m}\subseteq\mathcal{F}^{w_{1}}\subseteq\mathcal{F}^{1}.) From this filtration, we obtain the associated grading

𝕂​[x]=⨁k=0∞𝒫k,\displaystyle\mathbb{K}[x]=\bigoplus_{k=0}^{\infty}\mathcal{P}^{k},

where the 𝕂\mathbb{K}-vector space 𝒫k\mathcal{P}^{k} of quasi-homogeneous polynomials of weighted degree kk has basis given by {xα∣⟨w,α⟩=k}\{x^{\alpha}\mid\langle w,\alpha\rangle=k\}. For the trivial weighting w=(1,…,1)w=(1,\ldots,1), we recover the 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adic filtration on 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] (ℱk=𝔪k\mathcal{F}^{k}=\mathfrak{m}^{k}) and the usual grading on 𝕂​[x]\mathbb{K}[x] by polynomial homogeneity; the adjective “unweighted” shall always refer to this weighting. Given a power series f∈𝕂​[[x]]f\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!], we define

Θw​(f):=max⁡{k∣f∈ℱk}=min⁡{⟨w,α⟩∣cα≠0},\displaystyle\Theta_{w}(f):=\max\{k\mid f\in\mathcal{F}^{k}\}=\min\{\langle w,\alpha\rangle\mid c_{\alpha}\neq 0\},

with the convention that Θw​(0)=∞\Theta_{w}(0)=\infty. (For non-trivial weightings, we do not extend this “weighted Big Theta” notation to nn-tuples, as different components may have different weights.)

Example 3.2.

On 𝕂2\mathbb{K}^{2} with coordinates (x,y)(x,y), we may consider xx “quadratic” and yy “cubic” by assigning weights (w1,w2)=(2,3)(w_{1},w_{2})=(2,3). Then ℱ1=ℱ2=𝔪\mathcal{F}^{1}=\mathcal{F}^{2}=\mathfrak{m}, and the next few terms of the filtration on 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] have generating sets

ℱ3\displaystyle\mathcal{F}^{3} =⟨x2,y⟩,\displaystyle=\langle x^{2},y\rangle, ℱ4\displaystyle\mathcal{F}^{4} =⟨x2,x​y,y2⟩,\displaystyle=\langle x^{2},xy,y^{2}\rangle, ℱ5\displaystyle\mathcal{F}^{5} =⟨x3,x​y,y2⟩,\displaystyle=\langle x^{3},xy,y^{2}\rangle, ℱ6=⟨x3,x2​y,y2⟩.\displaystyle\mathcal{F}^{6}=\langle x^{3},x^{2}y,y^{2}\rangle.

For this weighting, there are no non-zero quasi-homogeneous polynomials of weighted degree 11.

So far, there has been a lot of algebra and not much calculus, so let us compare these to analogous definitions in the differentiable world. Recall that a function f:ℝn→ℝf\colon\mathbb{R}^{n}\to\mathbb{R} is called positively homogeneous of degree s∈ℝs\in\mathbb{R} if f​(t​x)=ts​f​(x)f(tx)=t^{s}f(x) for all x∈ℝnx\in\mathbb{R}^{n} and t>0t>0. For a function that is smooth at 0∈ℝn0\in\mathbb{R}^{n} and positively homogeneous of degree ss, one has the following trichotomy:

  • •

    If s<0s<0, then f≡0f\equiv 0.

  • •

    If s=0s=0, then ff is constant.

  • •

    If s>0s>0, then ss is an integer and ff is a homogeneous polynomial function of degree ss.

Thus, smooth positively homogeneous functions can be identified with homogeneous polynomials and studied purely algebraically. Introducing a weighting w=(w1,…,wn)w=(w_{1},\ldots,w_{n}) into the picture, let us call a function f:ℝn→ℝf\colon\mathbb{R}^{n}\to\mathbb{R} positively quasi-homogeneous of weighted degree s∈ℝs\in\mathbb{R} if κt∗​f=ts​f\kappa_{t}^{*}f=t^{s}f for all t>0t>0, where κt:ℝn→ℝn\kappa_{t}\colon\mathbb{R}^{n}\to\mathbb{R}^{n} is the linear map given by

κt​(x1,…,xn)=(tw1​x1,…,twn​xn).\displaystyle\kappa_{t}(x^{1},\ldots,x^{n})=(t^{w_{1}}x^{1},\ldots,t^{w_{n}}x^{n}). (7)

Are there any such functions that are smooth but not given by quasi-homogeneous polynomials? The answer is no: Grabowski–Rotkiewicz [12, Lemma 2.1] have shown that the trichotomy above continues to hold when one replaces “(positively) homogeneous” with “(positively) quasi-homogeneous” and “degree” with “weighted degree”. Thus, smooth positively quasi-homogeneous functions can also be studied through an algebraic lens.

The diffeomorphisms κt\kappa_{t} pull back the smooth vector field ∂∂xi\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} on ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n} to κt∗​∂∂xi=t−wi​∂∂xi\kappa_{t}^{*}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}=t^{-w_{i}}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}; mnemonically, one should think of ∂∂xi\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} as having weighted degree −wi-w_{i} “since xix^{i} is in the denominator”. Formally, the filtration on 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] induces a filtration on 𝔛\mathfrak{X} that starts in negative degrees and is compatible with Lie brackets:

𝔛=𝔛w​[[−wn]]⊇𝔛w​[[−wn+1]]⊇𝔛w​[[−wn+2]]⊇⋯,\displaystyle\mathfrak{X}=\mathfrak{X}_{w}[\![-w_{n}]\!]\supseteq\mathfrak{X}_{w}[\![-w_{n}+1]\!]\supseteq\mathfrak{X}_{w}[\![-w_{n}+2]\!]\supseteq\cdots,

where 𝔛w​[[k]]\mathfrak{X}_{w}[\![k]\!] is the 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]-submodule of formal vector fields that act on 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] by raising filtration degree by kk. Equivalently, 𝔛w​[[k]]\mathfrak{X}_{w}[\![k]\!] is generated by monomial vector fields xα​∂∂xix^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} such that ⟨w,α⟩−wi≥k\langle w,\alpha\rangle-w_{i}\geq k. Similarly, the associated grading on 𝕂​[x]\mathbb{K}[x] makes 𝔛pol\mathfrak{X}_{\mathrm{pol}} into a graded Lie algebra

𝔛pol=⨁k=−wn∞𝔛w​[k],\displaystyle\mathfrak{X}_{\mathrm{pol}}=\bigoplus_{k=-w_{n}}^{\infty}\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k],

where the subspace 𝔛w​[k]\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k] of quasi-homogeneous polynomial vector fields of weighted degree kk acts on 𝕂​[x]\mathbb{K}[x] by raising gradation degree by kk. Equivalently, 𝔛w​[k]\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k] has a basis given by monomial vector fields xα​∂∂xix^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} such that ⟨w,α⟩−wi=k\langle w,\alpha\rangle-w_{i}=k; thus

𝔛w​[k]≅⨁i=1n𝒫wi+k​ as ​𝕂​-vector spaces.\displaystyle\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k]\cong\bigoplus_{i=1}^{n}\mathcal{P}^{w_{i}+k}\text{ as }\mathbb{K}\text{-vector spaces}. (8)
Remark 3.3.

Alternatively, by interpreting the right-hand side of Equation (7) as an nn-tuple of formal power series, one obtains a 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra endomorphism κt\kappa_{t} of 𝕂​[[t,x]]\mathbb{K}[\![t,x]\!] such that κt​(t)=t\kappa_{t}(t)=t and κt​(xi)=twi​xi\kappa_{t}(x^{i})=t^{w_{i}}x^{i}. This is not a formal isotopy, as its inverse would have to map xi↦t−wi​xix^{i}\mapsto t^{-w_{i}}x^{i}. But if one regards κt\kappa_{t} as acting on Laurent series in tt with coefficients in 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] and suitably defines pullbacks in this setting, then 𝔛w​[k]\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k] is the set of polynomial vector fields XX satisfying κt∗​X=tk​X\kappa_{t}^{*}X=t^{k}X. (When 𝕂=ℝ\mathbb{K}=\mathbb{R}, this is equivalent to requiring κt∗​X=tk​X\kappa_{t}^{*}X=t^{k}X in the smooth sense for all t>0t>0 by Grabowski–Rotkiewicz.)

3.2 Weights and evaluation

We can now state and prove the promised weighted generalizations of Propositions 2.26 and 2.28.

Proposition 3.4.

Let φt\varphi_{t} be a formal isotopy of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} with corresponding nn-tuple written in the form φ​(t,x)=∑k=0∞tk​φk​(x)\varphi(t,x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}\varphi_{k}(x) where φk​(x)=(φk1​(x),…,φkn​(x))∈𝕂​[[x]]n\varphi_{k}(x)=\big(\varphi_{k}^{1}(x),\ldots,\varphi_{k}^{n}(x)\big)\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]^{n}.

If there exists a weighting w=(w1,…,wn)w=(w_{1},\ldots,w_{n}) of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} such that, for all i∈{1,…,n}i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}, we have

Θw​(φ0i)≥wi,\displaystyle\Theta_{w}(\varphi_{0}^{i})\geq w_{i}, Θw​(φki)≥wi+1​ for all ​k≥1,\displaystyle\Theta_{w}(\varphi_{k}^{i})\geq w_{i}+1\text{ for all }k\geq 1, limk→∞Θ​(φk)=∞,\displaystyle\lim_{k\to\infty}\Theta(\varphi_{k})=\infty,

then φt\varphi_{t} is evaluative.

Taking the trivial weighting w=(1,…,1)w=(1,\ldots,1) recovers Proposition 2.26 as the condition on φ0\varphi_{0} reduces to Θ​(φ0)≥1\Theta(\varphi_{0})\geq 1, which formal isotopies always satisfy (Remark 2.4). Note that the limit condition is stated with unweighted Big Theta notation for tuples, but it is equivalent to requiring that limk→∞Θw​(φki)=∞\lim_{k\to\infty}\Theta_{w}(\varphi_{k}^{i})=\infty for all ii since the filtration topology defined by ww coincides with the 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adic topology; both limits are equivalent to saying that limk→∞φki=0\lim_{k\to\infty}\varphi_{k}^{i}=0 in this topology for all ii.

Proof 3.5.

By definition, Θ​(φk)\Theta(\varphi_{k}) is the smallest ℓ∈ℕ\ell\in\mathbb{N} for which there exists i∈{1,…,n}i\in\{1,\ldots,n\} and α∈ℕn\alpha\in\mathbb{N}^{n} such that |α|=ℓ|\alpha|=\ell and the coefficient of xαx^{\alpha} in φki​(x)\varphi_{k}^{i}(x) is non-zero (unless φk​(x)=(0,…,0)\varphi_{k}(x)=(0,\ldots,0)). For any particular α\alpha, we have Θ​(φk)>|α|\Theta(\varphi_{k})>|\alpha| for sufficiently large kk since Θ​(φk)→∞\Theta(\varphi_{k})\to\infty. Thus the coefficient of xαx^{\alpha} in each φki​(x)\varphi_{k}^{i}(x) is non-zero for finitely many kk’s, so the coefficient of xαx^{\alpha} in φi​(t,x)=∑ktk​φki​(x)\varphi^{i}(t,x)=\sum_{k}t^{k}\varphi_{k}^{i}(x) is actually a polynomial in tt. As α\alpha was arbitrary, each φi\varphi^{i} is an evaluative power series.

Since Θw​(φki)≥wi≥1\Theta_{w}(\varphi_{k}^{i})\geq w_{i}\geq 1 for all k≥0k\geq 0, we have φki​(x)∈ℱwi⊆ℱ1=𝔪\varphi_{k}^{i}(x)\in\mathcal{F}^{w_{i}}\subseteq\mathcal{F}^{1}=\mathfrak{m}, hence

φ​(t,0)=∑k=0∞tk​φk​(0)=(0,…,0).\displaystyle\varphi(t,0)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}\varphi_{k}(0)=(0,\ldots,0).

Thus condition (i) of Definition 2.19 is satisfied, and we are left with checking condition (ii). We claim that D​φk​(0)D\varphi_{k}(0) is a block upper triangular matrix for all k≥0k\geq 0, and strictly block upper triangular for all k≥1k\geq 1; that is,

D​φk​(0)\displaystyle D\varphi_{k}(0) =(Ak,1∗⋯∗O⋱⋱⋮⋮⋱⋱∗O⋯OAk,m),where ​Ak,ℓ=O​ for ​k≥1.\displaystyle=\begin{pmatrix}A_{k,1}&*&\cdots&*\\ O&\ddots&\ddots&\vdots\\ \vdots&\ddots&\ddots&*\\ O&\cdots&O&A_{k,m}\end{pmatrix},\quad\text{where }A_{k,\ell}=O\text{ for }k\geq 1. (9)

Here, mm denotes the number of distinct weights and each Ak,ℓA_{k,\ell} is a dℓ×dℓd_{\ell}\times d_{\ell} matrix, where dℓd_{\ell} is the number of coordinates assigned to the ℓ\ellth smallest weight value.555For example, if w=(1,4,5,5)w=(1,4,5,5), then there are m=3m=3 distinct weights and (d1,d2,d3)=(1,1,2)(d_{1},d_{2},d_{3})=(1,1,2).

To prove the claim, recall that ∂∂xj\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{j}}} decreases filtration degree on 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] by wjw_{j}, so

Θw​(∂φki∂xj)≥Θw​(φki)−wj.\displaystyle\Theta_{w}\left(\frac{\partial\varphi_{k}^{i}}{\partial x^{j}}\right)\geq\Theta_{w}(\varphi_{k}^{i})-w_{j}.

Since we required weights to be sorted from smallest to largest in Definition 3.1 and the partitioning of D​φk​(0)D\varphi_{k}(0) illustrated in Equation (9) is along blocks corresponding to distinct weights, it follows that entries below the main block diagonal are those indexed by (i,j)(i,j) where wi>wjw_{i}>w_{j}. Since Θw​(φki)≥wi\Theta_{w}(\varphi_{k}^{i})\geq w_{i} for all k≥0k\geq 0, we thus have Θw​(∂φki∂xj)≥wi−wj>0\Theta_{w}\Big(\frac{\partial\varphi_{k}^{i}}{\partial x^{j}}\Big)\geq w_{i}-w_{j}>0, hence ∂φki∂xj​(0)=0\frac{\partial\varphi_{k}^{i}}{\partial x^{j}}(0)=0 below the main block diagonal. Entries within the main block diagonal are indexed by (i,j)(i,j) with wi=wjw_{i}=w_{j}, so if k≥1k\geq 1, then Θw​(∂φki∂xj)≥wi−wj+1=1\Theta_{w}\Big(\frac{\partial\varphi_{k}^{i}}{\partial x^{j}}\Big)\geq w_{i}-w_{j}+1=1, hence also ∂φki∂xj​(0)=0\frac{\partial\varphi_{k}^{i}}{\partial x^{j}}(0)=0. This proves the claim.

Now observe that Dx​φ​(t,x)=∑k=0∞tk​D​φk​(x)D_{x}\varphi(t,x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}D\varphi_{k}(x), so upon setting x1=⋯=xn=0x^{1}=\cdots=x^{n}=0, we obtain

Dx​φ​(t,0)\displaystyle D_{x}\varphi(t,0) =∑k=0∞tk​D​φk​(0)=D​φ0​(0)+∑k=1∞tk⋅(strictly block upper triangular matrices).\displaystyle=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}D\varphi_{k}(0)=D\varphi_{0}(0)+\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}t^{k}\cdot\text{(strictly block upper triangular matrices)}.

Consequently detDx​φ​(t,0)=∏ℓ=1mdet(A0,ℓ)=detD​φ0​(0)\det D_{x}\varphi(t,0)=\prod_{\ell=1}^{m}\det(A_{0,\ell})=\det D\varphi_{0}(0). As φt\varphi_{t} is a formal isotopy, detD​φ0​(0)\det D\varphi_{0}(0) is a non-zero element of 𝕂\mathbb{K} (Remark 2.4), so condition (ii) of Definition 2.19 is satisfied.

Proposition 3.6.

Suppose Xt∈𝔛tX_{t}\in\mathfrak{X}_{t} is written in the form Xt=∑k=0∞tk​XkX_{t}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}X_{k} where Xk∈𝔛X_{k}\in\mathfrak{X}. Identify the flow φt\varphi_{t} of XtX_{t} with an nn-tuple φ​(t,x)=∑k=0∞tk​φk​(x)\varphi(t,x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}\varphi_{k}(x) where φk​(x)∈𝕂​[[x]]n\varphi_{k}(x)\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]^{n}.

If there exists a weighting w=(w1,…,wn)w=(w_{1},\ldots,w_{n}) of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} such that Xk∈𝔛w​[[k+1]]X_{k}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[\![k+1]\!] for all k≥0k\geq 0, then with respect to this weighting, one has Θw​(φki)≥wi+k\Theta_{w}(\varphi_{k}^{i})\geq w_{i}+k for all i∈{1,…,n}i\in\{1,\ldots,n\} and k≥0k\geq 0. In particular, φt\varphi_{t} is an evaluative formal isotopy by Proposition 3.4.

Proof 3.7.

We proceed by (strong) induction on kk. By Theorem 2.8(IC), we have φ0i​(x)=xi\varphi_{0}^{i}(x)=x^{i}, hence Θw​(φ0i)=wi\Theta_{w}(\varphi_{0}^{i})=w_{i} for each i∈{1,…,n}i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}. This establishes the base case; for the inductive step, assume that Θw​(φki)≥wi+k\Theta_{w}(\varphi_{k}^{i})\geq w_{i}+k for all i∈{1,…,n}i\in\{1,\ldots,n\} and k≤mk\leq m. As observed in Remark 2.15, the coefficient of tmt^{m} on the left-hand side of Theorem 2.8(FP) when applied to each xix^{i} is (m+1)​φm+1i​(x)(m+1)\varphi_{m+1}^{i}(x); we want to find Θw\Theta_{w} of the corresponding coefficient of tmt^{m} on the right-hand side. Warning: an unglamorous calculation follows. The reader may wish to skip to the final expression (†\dagger ‣ 3.7) for this coefficient.

As Θw​(xi)=wi\Theta_{w}(x^{i})=w_{i} and XkX_{k} raises filtration degree by k+1k+1, we have Θw​(Xk​(xi))≥wi+k+1\Theta_{w}\big(X_{k}(x^{i})\big)\geq w_{i}+k+1. Thus

Xk​(xi)\displaystyle X_{k}(x^{i}) =∑α∈ℕn⟨w,α⟩≥wi+k+1ck,αi​xα,for some coefficients ​ck,αi∈𝕂.\displaystyle=\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}\alpha\in\mathbb{N}^{n}\\ \langle w,\alpha\rangle\geq w_{i}+k+1\end{subarray}}c_{k,\alpha}^{i}x^{\alpha},\quad\text{for some coefficients }c_{k,\alpha}^{i}\in\mathbb{K}.

As φt\varphi_{t} is a 𝕂\mathbb{K}-algebra automorphism fixing tt, we thus have

φt​(Xt​(xi))\displaystyle\varphi_{t}\big(X_{t}(x^{i})\big) =∑k=0∞tk​φt​(Xk​(xi))=∑k=0∞∑α∈ℕn⟨w,α⟩≥wi+k+1ck,αi​tk​∏j=1nφt​(xj)αj.\displaystyle=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}\varphi_{t}\big(X_{k}(x^{i})\big)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}\alpha\in\mathbb{N}^{n}\\ \langle w,\alpha\rangle\geq w_{i}+k+1\end{subarray}}c_{k,\alpha}^{i}t^{k}\prod_{j=1}^{n}\varphi_{t}(x^{j})^{\alpha_{j}}.

For each j∈{1,…,n}j\in\{1,\ldots,n\}, we can express φt​(xj)αj\varphi_{t}(x^{j})^{\alpha_{j}} as a sum over multi-indices of length αj\alpha_{j} like in the proof of Proposition 2.28:

φt​(xj)αj=(∑ℓ=0∞tℓ​φℓj​(x))αj=∑βj∈ℕαjt|βj|​∏ℓ=1αjφβℓjj​(x).\displaystyle\varphi_{t}(x^{j})^{\alpha_{j}}=\left(\sum_{\ell=0}^{\infty}t^{\ell}\varphi_{\ell}^{j}(x)\right)^{\alpha_{j}}=\sum_{\beta^{j}\in\mathbb{N}^{\alpha_{j}}}t^{|\beta^{j}|}\prod_{\ell=1}^{\alpha_{j}}\varphi_{\beta^{j}_{\ell}}^{j}(x).

The nn multi-indices βj∈ℕαj\beta^{j}\in\mathbb{N}^{\alpha_{j}} can be concatenated into a single multi-index β:=(β1,…,βn)\beta:=(\beta^{1},\ldots,\beta^{n}) of total length |α||\alpha|. Conversely, any β∈ℕ|α|≅ℕα1×⋯×ℕαn\beta\in\mathbb{N}^{|\alpha|}\cong\mathbb{N}^{\alpha_{1}}\times\cdots\times\mathbb{N}^{\alpha_{n}} splits into nn multi-indices βj∈ℕαj\beta^{j}\in\mathbb{N}^{\alpha_{j}}, where βj\beta^{j} is the part of β\beta indexed by Lαj:={ℓ∈ℕ∣α1+⋯+αj−1<ℓ≤α1+⋯+αj}L_{\alpha}^{j}:=\{\ell\in\mathbb{N}\mid\alpha_{1}+\cdots+\alpha_{j-1}<\ell\leq\alpha_{1}+\cdots+\alpha_{j}\}. Then

∏j=1nφt​(xj)αj\displaystyle\prod_{j=1}^{n}\varphi_{t}(x^{j})^{\alpha_{j}} =(∑β1∈ℕα1t|β1|​∏ℓ=1α1φβℓ11​(x))​⋯​(∑βn∈ℕαnt|βn|​∏ℓ=1αnφβℓnn​(x))\displaystyle=\left(\sum_{\beta^{1}\in\mathbb{N}^{\alpha_{1}}}t^{|\beta^{1}|}\prod_{\ell=1}^{\alpha_{1}}\varphi_{\beta^{1}_{\ell}}^{1}(x)\right)\cdots\left(\sum_{\beta^{n}\in\mathbb{N}^{\alpha_{n}}}t^{|\beta^{n}|}\prod_{\ell=1}^{\alpha_{n}}\varphi_{\beta^{n}_{\ell}}^{n}(x)\right)
=∑β1∈ℕα1⋯​∑βn∈ℕαnt|β1|+⋯+|βn|​∏j=1n∏ℓ=1αjφβℓjj​(x)\displaystyle=\sum_{\beta^{1}\in\mathbb{N}^{\alpha_{1}}}\cdots\sum_{\beta^{n}\in\mathbb{N}^{\alpha_{n}}}t^{|\beta^{1}|+\cdots+|\beta^{n}|}\prod_{j=1}^{n}\prod_{\ell=1}^{\alpha_{j}}\varphi_{\beta^{j}_{\ell}}^{j}(x)
=∑β∈ℕ|α|t|β|​∏j=1n∏ℓ∈Lαjφβℓj​(x).\displaystyle=\sum_{\beta\in\mathbb{N}^{|\alpha|}}t^{|\beta|}\prod_{j=1}^{n}\prod_{\ell\in L_{\alpha}^{j}}\varphi_{\beta_{\ell}}^{j}(x).

It follows that the coefficient of tmt^{m} on the right-hand side of Theorem 2.8(FP) is

∑k=0∞∑α∈ℕn⟨w,α⟩≥wi+k+1∑β∈ℕ|α||β|+k=mck,αi​∏j=1n∏ℓ∈Lαjφβℓj​(x).\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}\alpha\in\mathbb{N}^{n}\\ \langle w,\alpha\rangle\geq w_{i}+k+1\end{subarray}}\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}\beta\in\mathbb{N}^{|\alpha|}\\ |\beta|+k=m\end{subarray}}c^{i}_{k,\alpha}\prod_{j=1}^{n}\prod_{\ell\in L_{\alpha}^{j}}\varphi_{\beta_{\ell}}^{j}(x). (†\dagger)

The salient point is that for each fixed kk, α\alpha, and β\beta appearing in (†\dagger ‣ 3.7), we have βℓ≤|β|≤m\beta_{\ell}\leq|\beta|\leq m for each 1≤ℓ≤|α|1\leq\ell\leq|\alpha|, hence Θw​(φβℓj)≥wj+βℓ\Theta_{w}(\varphi_{\beta_{\ell}}^{j})\geq w_{j}+\beta_{\ell} by the induction hypothesis. Thus

Θw​(∏j=1n∏ℓ∈Lαjφβℓj)=∑j=1n∑ℓ∈LαjΘw​(φβℓj)≥∑j=1n∑ℓ∈Lαj(wj+βℓ).\displaystyle\Theta_{w}\Bigg(\prod_{j=1}^{n}\prod_{\ell\in L_{\alpha}^{j}}\varphi_{\beta_{\ell}}^{j}\Bigg)=\sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{\ell\in L_{\alpha}^{j}}\Theta_{w}(\varphi_{\beta_{\ell}}^{j})\geq\sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{\ell\in L_{\alpha}^{j}}(w_{j}+\beta_{\ell}).

Observing that

∑j=1n∑ℓ∈Lαjwj\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{\ell\in L_{\alpha}^{j}}w_{j} =∑j=1nwj​αj=⟨w,α⟩≥wi+k+1,\displaystyle=\sum_{j=1}^{n}w_{j}\alpha_{j}=\langle w,\alpha\rangle\geq w_{i}+k+1, ∑j=1n∑ℓ∈Lαjβℓ\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{\ell\in L_{\alpha}^{j}}\beta_{\ell} =|β|,\displaystyle=|\beta|,

we see that ∑j=1n∑ℓ∈Lαj(wj+βℓ)≥wi+k+1+|β|=wi+m+1\sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{\ell\in L_{\alpha}^{j}}(w_{j}+\beta_{\ell})\geq w_{i}+k+1+|\beta|=w_{i}+m+1. Thus Θw​(φm+1i)≥wi+m+1\Theta_{w}(\varphi_{m+1}^{i})\geq w_{i}+m+1, completing the induction.

Example 3.8.

As mentioned in Remark 2.27, the flow of y​∂∂xy\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}} is the formal isotopy of 𝕂2\mathbb{K}^{2} corresponding to the 22-tuple (x+t​y,y)(x+ty,y). We could not conclude that this is evaluative from either Propositions 2.26 or 2.28, but we can from Proposition 3.6 using any weighting (w1,w2)(w_{1},w_{2}) such that w1<w2w_{1}<w_{2}. The author does not know the answer to the following question: if a formal isotopy is evaluative, does there exist some weighting with respect to which the hypotheses of Proposition 3.4 hold (perhaps after permuting coordinates)?

4 Weighted linearization

4.1 Weighted linear approximations

Using the 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adic topology, every formal function f∈𝕂​[[x]]f\in\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] can be written as a convergent infinite sum of homogeneous polynomials (Equation (4)). As the filtration topology on 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] induced by a weighting w=(w1,…,wn)w=(w_{1},\ldots,w_{n}) coincides with the 𝔪\mathfrak{m}-adic topology, we can also write f=∑k=0∞fkf=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}f_{k} where fk∈𝒫kf_{k}\in\mathcal{P}^{k} for each kk. Time-independent formal vector fields X∈𝔛X\in\mathfrak{X} can similarly be written as an infinite sum of quasi-homogeneous polynomial vector fields:

X=∑k=−wn∞X[k],where ​X[k]∈𝔛w​[k].\displaystyle X=\sum_{k=-w_{n}}^{\infty}X_{[k]},\quad\text{where }X_{[k]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k].

Concretely, if X=∑iXi​∂∂xiX=\sum_{i}X^{i}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} where Xi​(x)=∑αcαi​xαX^{i}(x)=\sum_{\alpha}c^{i}_{\alpha}x^{\alpha}, then X[k]=∑iX[k]i​∂∂xiX_{[k]}=\sum_{i}X_{[k]}^{i}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} where

X[k]i​(x)=∑⟨w,α⟩−wi=kcαi​xα.\displaystyle X_{[k]}^{i}(x)=\sum_{\langle w,\alpha\rangle-w_{i}=k}c^{i}_{\alpha}x^{\alpha}.
Definition 4.1.

We call X[0]X_{[0]} the weighted linear approximation of XX. A formal vector field X∈𝔛X\in\mathfrak{X} is called ww-admissible if X∈𝔛w​[[0]]X\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[\![0]\!], or equivalently, if X[k]=0X_{[k]}=0 for all k<0k<0. We use the same terminology for a smooth vector field on an open neighbourhood of 0∈ℝn0\in\mathbb{R}^{n}, referring to its formal Taylor series at 0.

Equivalently, if one extends κt\kappa_{t} to act on Laurent series in tt like in Remark 3.3, then for any X∈𝔛X\in\mathfrak{X}, we can write the pullback of XX along κt\kappa_{t} as

κt∗​X=∑k=−wn∞tk​X[k].\displaystyle\kappa_{t}^{*}X=\sum_{k=-w_{n}}^{\infty}t^{k}X_{[k]}.

This is generally not a time-dependent formal vector field in the sense of Section 2.2 as the right-hand side can include negative powers of tt, but ww-admissible formal vector fields are precisely those for which κt∗​X\kappa_{t}^{*}X does define a time-dependent formal vector field. If XX is ww-admissible, then Xt:=κt∗​XX_{t}:=\kappa_{t}^{*}X is evaluative, with Xt|t=0=X[0]X_{t}|_{t=0}=X_{[0]} and Xt|t=1=XX_{t}|_{t=1}=X. (For smooth vector fields on ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n}, ww-admissibility means that limt→0+κt∗​X\lim_{t\to 0^{+}}\kappa_{t}^{*}X exists and equals X[0]X_{[0]}, where κt\kappa_{t} is the diffeomorphism given by Equation (7).) If XX is admissible with respect to any weighting, then its components XiX^{i} satisfy Xi​(0)=0X^{i}(0)=0 for each ii—another reason we require weights to be positive.

We now turn to the question of formal weighted linearizability for vector fields:

Given a ww-admissible vector field X∈𝔛w​[[0]]X\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[\![0]\!] with weighted linear approximation X[0]∈𝔛w​[0]X_{[0]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[0], when does there exist a formal diffeomorphism φ:𝕂​[[x]]→𝕂​[[x]]\varphi\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] such that φ∗​X=X[0]\varphi^{*}X=X_{[0]}?

Before giving a general answer, we note a particularly special case. Recall that the weighted Euler vector field associated to w=(w1,…,wn)w=(w_{1},\ldots,w_{n}) is given by

ℰw:=∑i=1nwi​xi​∂∂xi∈𝔛w​[0].\displaystyle\mathcal{E}_{w}:=\sum_{i=1}^{n}w_{i}x^{i}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[0].

For all monomials xαx^{\alpha}, one has ℰw​(xα)=⟨w,α⟩​xα\mathcal{E}_{w}(x^{\alpha})=\langle w,\alpha\rangle x^{\alpha} and [ℰw,xα​∂∂xi]=(⟨w,α⟩−wi)​xα​∂∂xi\left[\mathcal{E}_{w},x^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}\right]=(\langle w,\alpha\rangle-w_{i})x^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}, from which it follows that a polynomial f∈𝕂​[x]f\in\mathbb{K}[x] lies in 𝒫k\mathcal{P}^{k} if and only if ℰw​(f)=k​f\mathcal{E}_{w}(f)=kf, and a polynomial vector field X∈𝔛polX\in\mathfrak{X}_{\mathrm{pol}} lies in 𝔛w​[k]\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k] if and only if [ℰw,X]=k​X[\mathcal{E}_{w},X]=kX. A ww-admissible (formal or smooth) vector field XX is called weighted Euler-like if X[0]=ℰwX_{[0]}=\mathcal{E}_{w}. We obtain a formal version of [17, Lemma 2.5] over any field of characteristic zero:

Proposition 4.2.

If a formal vector field X∈𝔛X\in\mathfrak{X} is weighted Euler-like with respect to a weighting ww of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n}, then there exists a formal diffeomorphism φ\varphi of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} such that φ∗​X=ℰw\varphi^{*}X=\mathcal{E}_{w}.

Proof 4.3.

By assumption, we can write X=∑k=0∞X[k]X=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}X_{[k]} where X[k]∈𝔛w​[k]X_{[k]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k] and X[0]=ℰwX_{[0]}=\mathcal{E}_{w}. The time-dependent formal vector fields Xt,Ut∈𝔛tX_{t},U_{t}\in\mathfrak{X}_{t} given by Xt:=∑k=0∞tk​X[k]X_{t}:=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}X_{[k]} and Ut:=∑k=0∞tk​X[k+1]U_{t}:=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}X_{[k+1]} thus satisfy

[ℰw,Xt]\displaystyle[\mathcal{E}_{w},X_{t}] =∑k=0∞tk​[ℰw,X[k]]=∑k=0∞k​tk​X[k]=t​d​Xtd​t,\displaystyle=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}[\mathcal{E}_{w},X_{[k]}]=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}kt^{k}X_{[k]}=t\frac{dX_{t}}{dt},
Xt−ℰw\displaystyle X_{t}-\mathcal{E}_{w} =(ℰw+∑k=1∞tk​X[k])−ℰw=t​Ut.\displaystyle=\bigg(\mathcal{E}_{w}+\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}t^{k}X_{[k]}\bigg)-\mathcal{E}_{w}=tU_{t}.

It follows that t​(d​Xtd​t+[Ut,Xt])=0t\left(\frac{dX_{t}}{dt}+[U_{t},X_{t}]\right)=0, hence d​Xtd​t+[Ut,Xt]=0\frac{dX_{t}}{dt}+[U_{t},X_{t}]=0 as 𝔛t\mathfrak{X}_{t} is torsion-free. Denoting the flow of UtU_{t} by φt\varphi_{t}, we see from the (r,s)=(1,0)(r,s)=(1,0) case of Proposition 2.11 that

dd​t​(φt∗​Xt)=φt∗​(d​Xtd​t+[Ut,Xt])=0.\displaystyle\frac{d}{dt}(\varphi_{t}^{*}X_{t})=\varphi_{t}^{*}\left(\frac{dX_{t}}{dt}+[U_{t},X_{t}]\right)=0.

Now observe that XtX_{t} is evaluative, with Xt|t=0=ℰwX_{t}|_{t=0}=\mathcal{E}_{w} and Xt|t=1=XX_{t}|_{t=1}=X; on the other hand, φt\varphi_{t} is evaluative by Proposition 3.6. Thus the formal diffeomorphism φ:=φ1\varphi:=\varphi_{1} satisfies φ∗​X=ℰw\varphi^{*}X=\mathcal{E}_{w} by the formal Moser trick (Corollary 2.24).

To motivate the general answer, suppose that XX is a smooth vector field on ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n} and φ:ℝn→ℝn\varphi\colon\mathbb{R}^{n}\to\mathbb{R}^{n} is an actual diffeomorphism satisfying φ∗​X=X[0]\varphi^{*}X=X_{[0]} as smooth vector fields. To employ a Moser-type argument, we want to think of X[0]X_{[0]} and XX as time slices of some time-dependent vector field XtX_{t} at t=0t=0 and t=1t=1, respectively, and φ\varphi as the t=1t=1 time slice of a one-parameter family of diffeomorphisms φt\varphi_{t}. The discussion following Definition 4.1 suggests choosing the candidate Xt:=κt∗​XX_{t}:=\kappa_{t}^{*}X, where κt\kappa_{t} is the diffeomorphism of ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n} given by Equation (7): as κ1=idℝn\kappa_{1}=\operatorname{id}_{\mathbb{R}^{n}}, we have Xt|t=1=XX_{t}|_{t=1}=X, and ww-admissibility of XX means exactly that XtX_{t} extends smoothly to t=0t=0 by Xt|t=0=X[0]X_{t}|_{t=0}=X_{[0]}. If we define φt:=κt−1∘φ∘κt\varphi_{t}:=\kappa_{t}^{-1}\circ\varphi\circ\kappa_{t} for t>0t>0, then φ1=φ\varphi_{1}=\varphi and666Upper stars reverse order here because we are working with honest-to-goodness smooth maps in this motivating discussion, c.f. Remark 2.2.

φt∗​Xt=(κt∘φt)∗​X=(φ∘κt)∗​X=κt∗​(φ∗​X)=κt∗​X[0]=X[0],∀t>0,\displaystyle\varphi_{t}^{*}X_{t}=(\kappa_{t}\circ\varphi_{t})^{*}X=(\varphi\circ\kappa_{t})^{*}X=\kappa_{t}^{*}(\varphi^{*}X)=\kappa_{t}^{*}X_{[0]}=X_{[0]},\qquad\forall t>0,

the last equality using that X[0]∈𝔛w​[0]X_{[0]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[0]. As X[0]X_{[0]} is constant with respect to tt, taking a time derivative yields

d​Xtd​t+[Ut,Xt]=0,\displaystyle\frac{dX_{t}}{dt}+[U_{t},X_{t}]=0, (10)

where UtU_{t} is the time-dependent vector field generating φt\varphi_{t}. Now observe that {κt}t>0\{\kappa_{t}\}_{t>0} defines a representation of the multiplicative group ℝ+\mathbb{R}^{+} on ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n} as κs​t=κs∘κt\kappa_{st}=\kappa_{s}\circ\kappa_{t} for all s,t>0s,t>0; consequently, the one-parameter family of diffeomorphisms {φt}t>0\{\varphi_{t}\}_{t>0} enjoys the homogeneity property

φs​t=κt−1∘φs∘κt,∀s,t>0.\displaystyle\varphi_{st}=\kappa_{t}^{-1}\circ\varphi_{s}\circ\kappa_{t},\qquad\forall s,t>0.

For fixed tt, differentiating both sides of this equation with respect to ss yields t​Us​t=κt∗​UstU_{st}=\kappa_{t}^{*}U_{s}. Assuming UsU_{s} is analytic with respect to the time parameter, so that Us=∑k=0∞sk​U[k+1]U_{s}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}s^{k}U_{[k+1]} for some time-independent vector fields U[k+1]U_{[k+1]} (the shifted index to be explained imminently), we obtain

∑k=0∞sk​tk+1​U[k+1]=∑k=0∞sk​κt∗​U[k+1].\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}s^{k}t^{k+1}U_{[k+1]}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}s^{k}\kappa_{t}^{*}U_{[k+1]}.

Comparing the coefficients of sks^{k} on each side yields κt∗​U[k+1]=tk+1​U[k+1]\kappa_{t}^{*}U_{[k+1]}=t^{k+1}U_{[k+1]}; that is, U[k+1]∈𝔛w​[k+1]U_{[k+1]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k+1]. If we also assume that Xt=∑k=0∞tk​X[k]X_{t}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}X_{[k]} is analytic, then Equation (10) becomes

∑k=0∞tk​((k+1)​X[k+1]+∑i,j≥0i+j=k[U[i+1],X[j]])=0.\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}\Bigg((k+1)X_{[k+1]}+\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}i,j\geq 0\\ i+j=k\end{subarray}}\left[U_{[i+1]},X_{[j]}\right]\Bigg)=0.

Thus each coefficient of tkt^{k} vanishes, and we obtain a weighted version of the homological equation

[X[0],U[k+1]]=(k+1)​X[k+1]−∑i=0k−1[X[k−i],U[i+1]].\displaystyle\left[X_{[0]},U_{[k+1]}\right]=(k+1)X_{[k+1]}-\sum_{i=0}^{k-1}\left[X_{[k-i]},U_{[i+1]}\right]. (11)

Both sides are quasi-homogeneous polynomial vector fields of weighted degree k+1k+1. In particular, if the adjoint operator adX[0]:=[X[0],⋅]:𝔛w​[k+1]→𝔛w​[k+1]\operatorname{ad}_{X_{[0]}}:=[X_{[0]},\cdot]\colon\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k+1]\to\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k+1] is invertible, then Equation (11) provides a recursive formula that uniquely determines U[k+1]U_{[k+1]} in terms of U[1],…,U[k]U_{[1]},\ldots,U_{[k]}.

These heuristics assumed analyticity with respect to time, but in the formal world, we need not concern ourselves with such matters. We thus obtain:

Theorem 4.4.

Suppose that X∈𝔛w​[[0]]X\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[\![0]\!] is ww-admissible, with weighted linear approximation X[0]∈𝔛w​[0]X_{[0]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[0]. If adX[0]:𝔛w​[k]→𝔛w​[k]\operatorname{ad}_{X_{[0]}}\colon\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k]\to\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k] is invertible for all k≥1k\geq 1, then there exists a formal diffeomorphism φ\varphi such that φ∗​X=X[0]\varphi^{*}X=X_{[0]}.

Proof 4.5.

We simply reverse the argument above: as XX is ww-admissible, we may write X=∑k=0∞X[k]X=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}X_{[k]} where X[k]∈𝔛w​[k]X_{[k]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k], so that Xt:=∑k=0∞tk​X[k]∈𝔛tX_{t}:=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}X_{[k]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{t} is an evaluative formal vector field satisfying Xt|t=0=X[0]X_{t}|_{t=0}=X_{[0]} and Xt|t=1=XX_{t}|_{t=1}=X. Define a time-dependent formal vector field Ut:=∑k=0∞tk​U[k+1]U_{t}:=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}t^{k}U_{[k+1]} by declaring U[1]∈𝔛w​[1]U_{[1]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[1] to be the unique solution to [X[0],U[1]]=X[1][X_{[0]},U_{[1]}]=X_{[1]}, then recursively defining U[k+1]∈𝔛w​[k+1]U_{[k+1]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k+1] by inverting Equation (11). By construction, XtX_{t} and UtU_{t} satisfy Equation (10) in the formal sense. By Proposition 3.6, the flow φt\varphi_{t} of UtU_{t} is evaluative. The (r,s)=(1,0)(r,s)=(1,0) case of Proposition 2.11 yields

dd​t​(φt∗​Xt)=φt∗​(d​Xtd​t+[Ut,Xt])=0,\displaystyle\frac{d}{dt}(\varphi_{t}^{*}X_{t})=\varphi_{t}^{*}\left(\frac{dX_{t}}{dt}+[U_{t},X_{t}]\right)=0,

so the formal Moser trick (Corollary 2.24) yields the desired formal diffeomorphism φ:=φ1\varphi:=\varphi_{1}.

As a special case, we note that if XX is weighted Euler-like, then adX[0]=adℰw\operatorname{ad}_{X_{[0]}}=\operatorname{ad}_{\mathcal{E}_{w}} acts on 𝔛w​[k]\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k] by multiplication by kk. The unique solution to Equation (11) is then given by U[k+1]=X[k+1]U_{[k+1]}=X_{[k+1]}, exactly recovering the proof of Proposition 4.2. In general, Theorem 4.4 is advantageous as each 𝔛w​[k]\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k] is a finite-dimensional 𝕂\mathbb{K}-vector space (Equation (8)), so we can determine invertibility of the adjoint operator on 𝔛w​[k]\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k] by examining its eigenvalues; such is the topic of the next section.

4.2 The weighted homological equation

For this section, we fix a weighting w=(w1,…,wn)w=(w_{1},\ldots,w_{n}) of 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} once and for all. Keeping with the notation introduced in the proof of Proposition 3.4, we denote the number of distinct weights in ww by mm and the number of coordinates assigned to the ℓ\ellth smallest weight value by dℓd_{\ell}. We also define μ:{1,…,n}→{1,…,m}\mu\colon\{1,\dots,n\}\to\{1,\dots,m\} by the property that μ​(i)\mu(i) is the integer ℓ\ell such that wiw_{i} is the ℓ\ellth smallest weight value, so that dℓd_{\ell} is the cardinality of μ−1​(ℓ)\mu^{-1}(\ell). We identify the linear polynomials in 𝕂​[x]\mathbb{K}[x] with the dual space (𝕂n)∗(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*} and define a grading

(𝕂n)∗=⨁ℓ=1mVℓ,\displaystyle(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*}=\bigoplus_{\ell=1}^{m}V^{\ell},

where VℓV^{\ell} is the subspace of (𝕂n)∗(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*} with basis given by {xi∣μ​(i)=ℓ}\{x^{i}\mid\mu(i)=\ell\}, so that Vμ​(i)=𝒫wi∩(𝕂n)∗V^{\mu(i)}=\mathcal{P}^{w_{i}}\cap(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*}.

Given a (smooth or formal) vector field XX on 𝕂n\mathbb{K}^{n} that vanishes at x=0x=0, we denote by Xlin∈𝔛polX_{\mathrm{lin}}\in\mathfrak{X}_{\mathrm{pol}} the usual linear approximation of XX, i.e., its weighted linear approximation with respect to the trivial weighting. As a derivation of 𝕂​[x]\mathbb{K}[x], XlinX_{\mathrm{lin}} preserves (unweighted) polynomial degree, hence leaves (𝕂n)∗(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*} invariant. As explained in Section 1, when 𝕂=ℝ\mathbb{K}=\mathbb{R} or 𝕂=ℂ\mathbb{K}=\mathbb{C}, conditions guaranteeing XX to be linearizable in a given category are often phrased in terms of the (possibly complex) eigenvalues of the restriction Xlin:(𝕂n)∗→(𝕂n)∗X_{\mathrm{lin}}\colon(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*}\to(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*}, whose matrix representation in the ordered basis {x1,…,xn}\{x^{1},\ldots,x^{n}\} is given by the transpose of the Jacobian matrix D​X​(0)DX(0) when XX is identified with the nn-tuple of its components.

If XX is ww-admissible with weighted linear approximation X[0]∈𝔛w​[0]X_{[0]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[0], then we would like to phrase analogous conditions for weighted linearizability in terms of “the eigenvalues of X[0]X_{[0]} on (𝕂n)∗(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*}”. A priori, it is not immediately clear how to interpret this, as X[0]X_{[0]} will generally not map (𝕂n)∗(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*} to itself: X[0]X_{[0]} preserves weighted polynomial degree, so the image of each xix^{i} under X[0]X_{[0]} may include products of unweighted linear polynomials with weighted degree equal to wiw_{i}. One way to return to (𝕂n)∗(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*} is to simply drop the (unweighted) non-linear terms—in other words, to act on (𝕂n)∗(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*} via (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}}. Alternatively, one could first act on (𝕂n)∗(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*} via the usual linear approximation XlinX_{\mathrm{lin}}, then drop any terms that are not weighted-linear—in other words, act on (𝕂n)∗(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*} via (Xlin)[0](X_{\mathrm{lin}})_{[0]}. It turns out that the order does not matter:

Proposition 4.6.

Suppose X∈𝔛X\in\mathfrak{X} has weighted linear approximation X[0]∈𝔛w​[0]X_{[0]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[0] and unweighted linear approximation Xlin∈𝔛polX_{\mathrm{lin}}\in\mathfrak{X}_{\mathrm{pol}}.

  1. (i)

    Weighted linear approximation commutes with unweighted linear approximation:

    (Xlin)[0]=(X[0])lin.\displaystyle(X_{\mathrm{lin}})_{[0]}=(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}}.
  2. (ii)

    If XX is ww-admissible, then (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} and XlinX_{\mathrm{lin}} have the same characteristic polynomial as operators on (𝕂n)∗(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*}, and (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} preserves the grading (𝕂n)∗=⨁ℓ=1mVℓ(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*}=\bigoplus_{\ell=1}^{m}V^{\ell}.

Proof 4.7.

(i) By definition, XlinX_{\mathrm{lin}} is obtained from XX by keeping only the monomial vector fields xα​∂∂xix^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} with |α|=1|\alpha|=1, whereas X[0]X_{[0]} is obtained from XX by keeping only the monomial vector fields with ⟨w,α⟩=wi\langle w,\alpha\rangle=w_{i}. Thus (Xlin)[0](X_{\mathrm{lin}})_{[0]} and (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} are both obtained from XX by keeping only the terms satisfying both conditions, hence they are equal.

(ii) Since XX is ww-admissible, if an (unweighted) linear term xj​∂∂xix^{j}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} appears in XX, then it must be the case that wj≥wiw_{j}\geq w_{i}. As weights are listed from smallest to largest, it follows that the Jacobian matrix D​X​(0)DX(0) is block upper triangular:

D​X​(0)=(A1∗⋯∗O⋱⋱⋮⋮⋱⋱∗O⋯OAm),\displaystyle DX(0)=\begin{pmatrix}A_{1}&*&\cdots&*\\ O&\ddots&\ddots&\vdots\\ \vdots&\ddots&\ddots&*\\ O&\cdots&O&A_{m}\end{pmatrix},

where each AℓA_{\ell} is a dℓ×dℓd_{\ell}\times d_{\ell} matrix. In the weighted linear approximation X[0]X_{[0]}, one deletes the terms xj​∂∂xix^{j}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} such that wj>wiw_{j}>w_{i}, hence the Jacobian matrix D​X[0]​(0)DX_{[0]}(0) is obtained from D​X​(0)DX(0) by replacing the blocks above the diagonal with zeroes. Thus D​X​(0)DX(0) and D​X[0]​(0)DX_{[0]}(0) have the same characteristic polynomial. The first claim follows as the transposes of these matrices respectively represent XlinX_{\mathrm{lin}} and (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} in the ordered basis {x1,…,xn}\{x^{1},\ldots,x^{n}\} of (𝕂n)∗(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*}; the second claim follows as we have just shown that D​X[0]​(0)DX_{[0]}(0) is block diagonal, and (the transposes of) these diagonal blocks correspond precisely to how (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} acts on the subspaces VℓV^{\ell}.

For simplicity, we henceforth assume that this characteristic polynomial splits in 𝕂\mathbb{K}. If not, then pass to its splitting field; the modifications needed are straightforward. As (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} respects the grading on (𝕂n)∗(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*}, we also require its eigenvalues to be listed in a way that respects this grading:

Definition 4.8.

A ww-compatible ordering is an ordering of the eigenvalues λ=(λ1,…,λn)\lambda=(\lambda_{1},\ldots,\lambda_{n}) of (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} such that each λi\lambda_{i} is an eigenvalue of the restriction (X[0])lin:Vμ​(i)→Vμ​(i)(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}}\colon V^{\mu(i)}\to V^{\mu(i)}.

We emphasize that the eigenvalues of (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} are the same as that of the usual linear approximation by Proposition 4.6(ii). The weights only matter insofar as determining what orderings are allowed in our generalization of the Poincaré–Sternberg non-resonance condition:

Definition 4.9.

Let λ=(λ1,…,λn)∈𝕂n\lambda=(\lambda_{1},\ldots,\lambda_{n})\in\mathbb{K}^{n} be a vector. A resonance with respect to ww is a pair (i,α)(i,\alpha) where i∈{1,…,n}i\in\{1,\ldots,n\} and α∈ℕn\alpha\in\mathbb{N}^{n} are such that

⟨λ,α⟩=λi,\displaystyle\langle\lambda,\alpha\rangle=\lambda_{i}, ⟨w,α⟩>wi.\displaystyle\langle w,\alpha\rangle>w_{i}.

If such a resonance exists, then the positive integer ⟨w,α⟩−wi\langle w,\alpha\rangle-w_{i} is called its weighted degree.

Given a ww-admissible formal vector field X∈𝔛w​[[0]]X\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[\![0]\!] with weighted linear approximation X[0]∈𝔛w​[0]X_{[0]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[0] and an integer k≥1k\geq 1, we say XX is non-resonant of weighted degree kk if some (equivalently, every) ww-compatible ordering of the eigenvalues of (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} has no resonances of weighted degree kk. We call XX non-resonant with respect to ww if this holds for all k≥1k\geq 1.

The trivial weighting recovers the usual definitions of (non-)resonance, for in this case the grading on (𝕂n)∗(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*} is trivial, so every ordering of the eigenvalues is a compatible ordering. We now state the main result of this section: the formal half of Theorem 1.1.

Theorem 4.10.

Let X∈𝔛w​[[0]]X\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[\![0]\!] be a ww-admissible formal vector field with weighted linear approximation X[0]∈𝔛w​[0]X_{[0]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[0]. If k≥1k\geq 1 is such that XX is non-resonant of weighted degree kk, then adX[0]:𝔛w​[k]→𝔛w​[k]\operatorname{ad}_{X_{[0]}}\colon\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k]\to\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k] is invertible. In particular, if XX is non-resonant with respect to ww, then there exists a formal diffeomorphism φ\varphi such that φ∗​X=X[0]\varphi^{*}X=X_{[0]} by Theorem 4.4.

Proof 4.11.

By Proposition 4.6(ii), each VℓV^{\ell} is invariant under (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}}. Concatenating Jordan bases for its restrictions to each VℓV^{\ell} produces an ordered Jordan basis {p1,…,pn}\{p^{1},\ldots,p^{n}\} of (𝕂n)∗(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*} for (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} with the property that pi∈Vμ​(i)=𝒫wi∩(𝕂n)∗p^{i}\in V^{\mu(i)}=\mathcal{P}^{w_{i}}\cap(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*} for each ii. Consequently, the linear change of basis xi↦pix^{i}\mapsto p^{i} extends to a formal diffeomorphism ψ:𝕂​[[x]]→𝕂​[[x]]\psi\colon\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!]\to\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] that leaves 𝕂​[x]\mathbb{K}[x] invariant and preserves both weighted and unweighted polynomial degree.777Grabowski–Rotkiewicz call a map preserving weighted polynomial degree a morphism of graded spaces. The same facts are true of φ:=ψ−1\varphi:=\psi^{-1}. Thus, pullback of formal vector fields along φ\varphi leaves 𝔛pol\mathfrak{X}_{\mathrm{pol}} invariant and preserves both the grading induced by ww and the usual unweighted grading on 𝔛pol\mathfrak{X}_{\mathrm{pol}}. As φ∗​X[0]∈𝔛w​[0]\varphi^{*}X_{[0]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[0] and adφ∗​X[0]=φ∗∘adX[0]∘(φ∗)−1\operatorname{ad}_{\varphi^{*}X_{[0]}}=\varphi^{*}\circ\operatorname{ad}_{X_{[0]}}\circ(\varphi^{*})^{-1}, it suffices to prove that adφ∗​X[0]\operatorname{ad}_{\varphi^{*}X_{[0]}} is invertible on 𝔛w​[k]\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k].

All monomial vector fields xβ​∂∂xjx^{\beta}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{j}}} appearing in X[0]X_{[0]} satisfy ⟨w,β⟩=wj\langle w,\beta\rangle=w_{j}. Split X[0]X_{[0]} into those with |β|=1|\beta|=1 and those with |β|>1|\beta|>1:

X[0]=(X[0])lin+(X[0]−(X[0])lin).\displaystyle X_{[0]}=(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}}+\left(X_{[0]}-(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}}\right).

As {p1,…,pn}\{p^{1},\ldots,p^{n}\} puts (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} into Jordan canonical form, we have

φ∗​(X[0])lin=λ1​x1​∂∂x1+∑j=2n(λj​xj+εj​xj−1)​∂∂xj,\displaystyle\varphi^{*}(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}}=\lambda_{1}x^{1}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{1}}}+\sum_{j=2}^{n}(\lambda_{j}x^{j}+\varepsilon_{j}x^{j-1})\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{j}}},

where (λ1,…,λn)(\lambda_{1},\ldots,\lambda_{n}) is a ww-compatible ordering of the eigenvalues of (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} and εj∈{0,1}\varepsilon_{j}\in\{0,1\} corresponds to whether pjp^{j} is an eigenvector or a generalized eigenvector of higher rank. Thus we can write φ∗​X[0]=Λ+J+T\varphi^{*}X_{[0]}=\Lambda+J+T, where

Λ\displaystyle\Lambda :=∑j=1nλj​xj​∂∂xj,\displaystyle:=\sum_{j=1}^{n}\lambda_{j}x^{j}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{j}}}, J\displaystyle J :=∑j=2nεj​xj−1​∂∂xj,\displaystyle:=\sum_{j=2}^{n}\varepsilon_{j}x^{j-1}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{j}}}, T\displaystyle T :=φ∗​(X[0]−(X[0])lin).\displaystyle:=\varphi^{*}\left(X_{[0]}-(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}}\right).

Observe that the adjoint operator associated to Λ\Lambda satisfies

adΛ⁡(xα​∂∂xi)\displaystyle\operatorname{ad}_{\Lambda}\left(x^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}\right) =(⟨λ,α⟩−λi)​xα​∂∂xi,\displaystyle=\big(\langle\lambda,\alpha\rangle-\lambda_{i}\big)x^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}},

so each xα​∂∂xix^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} is an eigenvector of adΛ\operatorname{ad}_{\Lambda} with eigenvalue ⟨λ,α⟩−λi\langle\lambda,\alpha\rangle-\lambda_{i}. We claim that adJ\operatorname{ad}_{J} and adT\operatorname{ad}_{T} are both represented by strictly upper triangular matrices when the basis of monomial vector fields {xα​∂∂xi∣⟨w,α⟩−wi=k}\left\{x^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}\mid\langle w,\alpha\rangle-w_{i}=k\right\} of 𝔛w​[k]\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k] is ordered as follows:

  1. 1.

    First, sort the monomial vector fields xα​∂∂xix^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} in decreasing order of |α||\alpha|.

  2. 2.

    Among those xα​∂∂xix^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} with a fixed value of |α||\alpha|, sort the ones with ∂∂xi\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} before the ones with ∂∂xj\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{j}}} if i>ji>j.

  3. 3.

    Among those xα​∂∂xix^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} with fixed values of |α||\alpha| and ii, sort the xαx^{\alpha}’s in decreasing lexicographical order of the multi-indices α\alpha.

From this claim, it follows that adφ∗​X[0]=adΛ+adJ+adT\operatorname{ad}_{\varphi^{*}X_{[0]}}=\operatorname{ad}_{\Lambda}+\operatorname{ad}_{J}+\operatorname{ad}_{T} is represented by an upper triangular matrix whose diagonal entries are of the form ⟨λ,α⟩−λi\langle\lambda,\alpha\rangle-\lambda_{i} where ⟨w,α⟩−wi=k\langle w,\alpha\rangle-w_{i}=k. As XX is non-resonant of weighted degree kk, these diagonal entries are non-zero, so adφ∗​X[0]\operatorname{ad}_{\varphi^{*}X_{[0]}} is invertible on 𝔛w​[k]\mathfrak{X}_{w}[k].

For adJ\operatorname{ad}_{J}, let j∈{2,…,n}j\in\{2,\ldots,n\} be such that εj=1\varepsilon_{j}=1. (This is only possible if wj−1=wjw_{j-1}=w_{j}; otherwise JJ would have a term xj−1​∂∂xjx^{j-1}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{j}}} with negative weighted degree.) We compute

[xj−1​∂∂xj,xα​∂∂xi]\displaystyle\left[x^{j-1}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{j}}},x^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}\right] =xj−1​∂xα∂xj​∂∂xi−xα​∂xj−1∂xi​∂∂xj.\displaystyle=x^{j-1}\frac{\partial x^{\alpha}}{\partial x^{j}}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}-x^{\alpha}\frac{\partial x^{j-1}}{\partial x^{i}}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{j}}}.

If the first term is non-zero, then ∂xα∂xj≠0\frac{\partial x^{\alpha}}{\partial x^{j}}\neq 0; writing α=(α1,…,αn)\alpha=(\alpha_{1},\ldots,\alpha_{n}), this implies that αj>0\alpha_{j}>0. Then

xj−1​∂xα∂xj=αj​xα′,where ​α′=(α1,…,αj−1+1,αj−1,…,αn).\displaystyle x^{j-1}\frac{\partial x^{\alpha}}{\partial x^{j}}=\alpha_{j}x^{\alpha^{\prime}},\qquad\text{where }\alpha^{\prime}=(\alpha_{1},\ldots,\alpha_{j-1}+1,\alpha_{j}-1,\ldots,\alpha_{n}).

As α′\alpha^{\prime} is lexicographically greater than α\alpha, the monomial vector field xα′​∂∂xix^{\alpha^{\prime}}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} comes before xα​∂∂xix^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} in the ordered basis. If the second term is non-zero, then i=j−1i=j-1, in which case xα​∂xj−1∂xi​∂∂xj=xα​∂∂xi+1x^{\alpha}\frac{\partial x^{j-1}}{\partial x^{i}}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{j}}}=x^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i+1}}}, which also comes before xα​∂∂xix^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} in the prescribed order.

For adT\operatorname{ad}_{T}, recall that X[0]−(X[0])linX_{[0]}-(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} only involves monomial vector fields xβ​∂∂xjx^{\beta}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{j}}} such that |β|>1|\beta|>1. As φ\varphi preserves unweighted polynomial degree, the same is true of T=φ∗​(X[0]−(X[0])lin)T=\varphi^{*}\big(X_{[0]}-(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}}\big). (In fact, since weights are strictly positive and listed from smallest to largest, the jjth component of both vector fields are polynomials in the previous variables x1,…,xj−1x^{1},\ldots,x^{j-1}, and only those with weight strictly less than wjw_{j}.) Thus [T,xα​∂∂xi][T,x^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}}] is a linear combination of monomial vector fields xγ​∂∂xkx^{\gamma}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{k}}} such that |γ|>|α||\gamma|>|\alpha|, all of which come before xα​∂∂xix^{\alpha}\frac{\partial}{\partial{x^{i}}} in the order described above.

Example 4.12.

Assign the weighting w=(1,2,2,3,3)w=(1,2,2,3,3) to the coordinates (x,y,z,u,v)(x,y,z,u,v) of 𝕂5\mathbb{K}^{5}. For arbitrary coefficients a,b,c,d,e,f,g,h∈𝕂a,b,c,d,e,f,g,h\in\mathbb{K}, the vector field

X[0]\displaystyle X_{[0]} =x​∂∂x+(a​x2+4​y+z)​∂∂y+(b​x2−4​y)​∂∂z\displaystyle=x\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}}+(ax^{2}+4y+z)\frac{\partial}{\partial{y}}+(bx^{2}-4y)\frac{\partial}{\partial{z}}
+(c​x3+d​x​y+e​x​z+4​u−v)​∂∂u+(f​x3+g​x​y+h​x​z+u+2​v)​∂∂v\displaystyle\qquad+(cx^{3}+dxy+exz+4u-v)\frac{\partial}{\partial{u}}+(fx^{3}+gxy+hxz+u+2v)\frac{\partial}{\partial{v}}

is quasi-homogeneous of weighted degree 0, and its unweighted linear approximation is

(X[0])lin=x​∂∂x+(4​y+z)​∂∂y−4​y​∂∂z+(4​u−v)​∂∂u+(u+2​v)​∂∂v.\displaystyle(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}}=x\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}}+(4y+z)\frac{\partial}{\partial{y}}-4y\frac{\partial}{\partial{z}}+(4u-v)\frac{\partial}{\partial{u}}+(u+2v)\frac{\partial}{\partial{v}}.

A ww-compatible ordering of the eigenvalues of (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} is given by λ=(1,2,2,3,3)\lambda=(1,2,2,3,3), and a corresponding ordered Jordan basis of (𝕂n)∗(\mathbb{K}^{n})^{*} is given by {x,2​y+z,y,u−v,u}\{x,2y+z,y,u-v,u\}. Thus the formal diffeomorphism ψ\psi from the proof of Theorem 4.10 and its inverse φ\varphi respectively act on 𝕂​[[x]]\mathbb{K}[\![x]\!] by

ψ:{x↦xy↦2​y+zz↦yu↦u−vv↦u\displaystyle\psi\colon\begin{cases}x\mapsto x\\ y\mapsto 2y+z\\ z\mapsto y\\ u\mapsto u-v\\ v\mapsto u\end{cases} φ:{x↦xy↦zz↦y−2​zu↦vv↦−u+v\displaystyle\varphi\colon\begin{cases}x\mapsto x\\ y\mapsto z\\ z\mapsto y-2z\\ u\mapsto v\\ v\mapsto-u+v\end{cases}

and it is straightforward to verify that

φ∗​((X[0])lin)=x​∂∂x+2​y​∂∂y+(y+2​z)​∂∂z+3​u​∂∂u+(u+3​v)​∂∂v\displaystyle\varphi^{*}\left((X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}}\right)=x\frac{\partial}{\partial{x}}+2y\frac{\partial}{\partial{y}}+(y+2z)\frac{\partial}{\partial{z}}+3u\frac{\partial}{\partial{u}}+(u+3v)\frac{\partial}{\partial{v}}

is in Jordan canonical form. It is a happy coincidence that λ=w\lambda=w; this implies that no i∈{1,…,n}i\in\{1,\ldots,n\} and α∈ℕn\alpha\in\mathbb{N}^{n} satisfy both ⟨λ,α⟩=λi\langle\lambda,\alpha\rangle=\lambda_{i} and ⟨w,α⟩>wi\langle w,\alpha\rangle>w_{i}, so X[0]X_{[0]} is non-resonant with respect to ww. Thus, any ww-admissible vector field whose weighted linear approximation is equal to X[0]X_{[0]} is related to X[0]X_{[0]} via a formal diffeomorphism by Theorem 4.10.

4.3 Weighted linearization of smooth vector fields

We conclude by returning to our original motivating question for developing the formal machinery for Moser’s trick. Let XX be a smooth vector field on an open neighbourhood of 0∈ℝn0\in\mathbb{R}^{n}. If XX is admissible and non-resonant with respect to a weighting ww and has weighted linear approximation X[0]∈𝔛w​[0]X_{[0]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[0], then there exists a formal diffeomorphism φ\varphi of ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n} such that φ∗​X=X[0]\varphi^{*}X=X_{[0]} by Theorem 4.10. To upgrade φ\varphi to an actual diffeomorphism using Chen’s theorem of equivalence [6], one would like to know if non-resonance with respect to ww implies hyperbolicity. This is indeed the case, but it is not immediately clear as the definition of weighted resonance involves ww-compatible orderings. Our final results compose the smooth half of Theorem 1.1.

Proposition 4.13.

Let XX be a smooth vector field on an open neighbourhood of 0∈ℝn0\in\mathbb{R}^{n}. If there exists a weighting ww of ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n} with respect to which XX is admissible and non-resonant, then 0 is a hyperbolic fixed point of XX.

Proof 4.14.

Suppose that 0 is not a hyperbolic fixed point, ww is any weighting with respect to which XX is admissible, and λ=(λ1,…,λn)\lambda=(\lambda_{1},\ldots,\lambda_{n}) is any ww-compatible ordering of the (possibly complex) eigenvalues of (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}}. If some eigenvalue λj\lambda_{j} vanishes, then λj=2​λj\lambda_{j}=2\lambda_{j} is a resonance with respect to ww as 2​wj>wj2w_{j}>w_{j}. If not, then there is a complex conjugate pair of non-zero purely imaginary eigenvalues ±i​θ\pm i\theta. By Proposition 4.6(ii), (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} preserves the grading (ℝn)∗=⨁ℓ=1mVℓ(\mathbb{R}^{n})^{*}=\bigoplus_{\ell=1}^{m}V^{\ell}, so this pair must come from the restriction of (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} to some VℓV^{\ell}: if any such pair came from two different VℓV^{\ell}’s, then the characteristic polynomial of some restriction of (X[0])lin(X_{[0]})_{\mathrm{lin}} to one of the VℓV^{\ell}’s would have an odd number of non-real roots when counted with multiplicity, which is impossible for a polynomial with real coefficients. Thus i​θ=2​(i​θ)+1​(−i​θ)i\theta=2(i\theta)+1(-i\theta) is a resonance with respect to ww.

Corollary 4.15.

Let XX be a smooth vector field on an open neighbourhood of 0∈ℝn0\in\mathbb{R}^{n}. For any weighting ww of ℝn\mathbb{R}^{n} with respect to which XX is admissible and non-resonant, there exists a germ of a diffeomorphism φ\varphi fixing 0 such that φ∗​X=X[0]\varphi^{*}X=X_{[0]}, where X[0]∈𝔛w​[0]X_{[0]}\in\mathfrak{X}_{w}[0] is the weighted linear approximation of XX with respect to ww.

Proof 4.16.

Apply Theorem 4.10, Proposition 4.13, and Chen’s theorem of equivalence.

Experimental support, please view the build logs for errors. Generated by L A T E xml .

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.

Have a free development cycle? Help support accessibility at arXiv! Our collaborators at LaTeXML maintain a list of packages that need conversion, and welcome developer contributions.

BETA
